proposals.title: Stochastic Mirror Descent on Spectrahedra: Algorithms and Convergence for Matrix-Monotonic Optimization
proposals.motivation: Optimization of matrix-monotonic functions under linear constraints is central to signal processing (e.g., MIMO beamforming, covariance estimation) and network resource allocation. However, existing deterministic methods do not scale to modern datasets, and standard stochastic first-order methods cannot handle complex matrix constraints like affine eigenvalue inequalities or linear operator constraints efficiently. This gap limits the application of these powerful models to streaming or large-scale data scenarios prevalent in communications and learning.
proposals.hypothesis: By exploiting the geodesic convexity of matrix-monotonic functions on suitable matrix manifolds, one can design stochastic first-order algorithms with provable convergence guarantees for problems with general linear constraints, achieving scalability comparable to Euclidean stochastic gradient descent while respecting the problem's intrinsic geometry.
proposals.method: We will develop a stochastic mirror descent framework where the mirror map is derived from the Legendre-Fenchel conjugate of a matrix entropy function, naturally enforcing constraints. For affine eigenvalue constraints, the algorithm will operate in the spectral domain using a von Neumann entropy Bregman divergence. For linear operator constraints, we will employ stochastic proximal operators within a Riemannian optimization scheme. Convergence rates will be analyzed under standard stochastic oracle models, focusing on expected optimality gap and constraint satisfaction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first unified stochastic optimization theory for matrix-monotonic problems with linear constraints, extending the scalability of stochastic methods to structured matrix manifolds. It will enable novel applications in online adaptive filter design for massive MIMO and large-scale, structurally-constrained covariance estimation from streaming data.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of stochastic approximation with the non-Euclidean geometry of matrix-monotonic functions under general linear constraints is unexplored. Moving beyond simple positive semidefinite cone projections to handle sampled or stochastic constraints within the algorithm's core update is a significant departure from prior deterministic or unconstrained stochastic methods.

proposals.title: Matrix-Monotonic Stochastic Mirror Descent: A Replicator Dynamics Analogy for Online Optimization on the Positive Definite Cone
proposals.motivation: Online optimization over the positive definite (PD) cone is central to adaptive covariance estimation, metric learning, and robust beamforming in wireless networks. Standard projected stochastic gradient descent is computationally expensive for PD constraints and ignores the manifold's intrinsic geometry. Drawing an analogy to replicator dynamics in theoretical ecology, which efficiently evolves distributions on a simplex via multiplicative updates, offers a principled path to scalable, geometry-aware algorithms for matrix domains.
proposals.hypothesis: A stochastic mirror descent framework, using a Bregman divergence defined by a matrix-monotonic function (e.g., log-det), will yield a multiplicative, exponentiated gradient update that maintains the PD constraint without projection, exploits geodesic convexity, and achieves superior regret or convergence rates in online settings compared to Euclidean methods.
proposals.method: We will formalize the analogy between replicator dynamics on the simplex and multiplicative updates on the PD cone via the matrix exponential map. The core algorithm will be a stochastic mirror descent iteration: X_{t+1} = ∇Φ*(∇Φ(X_t) - η_t G_t), where Φ is a matrix-monotonic potential (e.g., Φ(X)=Tr(X log X - X)), G_t is a stochastic subgradient, and ∇Φ* is the conjugate gradient. We will analyze its regret for online convex optimization and implement it for specific problems like online covariance estimation for portfolio optimization and adaptive precoding in MIMO systems, leveraging efficient eigenvalue decomposition for updates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, biologically-inspired algorithmic framework for online PD optimization that is projection-free and geometry-aware. It will deliver theoretical regret guarantees and demonstrate practical efficiency gains in communication and learning applications, bridging ideas from theoretical ecology, optimization, and signal processing.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious transfer of replicator dynamics and discrete-time multiplicative weights from the probability simplex to the full PD cone, and the systematic use of matrix-monotonic functions to generate novel Bregman divergences for stochastic mirror descent in this setting.

proposals.title: Evolutionary-Guided Stochastic Optimization for Large-Scale Structured Matrix Problems
proposals.motivation: Large-scale optimization problems with matrix-monotonic structure, prevalent in massive MIMO, intelligent metasurface configuration, and high-dimensional learning, are critical for next-generation wireless networks. Standard stochastic gradient descent (SGD) is sensitive to hyperparameters and often fails to exploit latent problem structure efficiently, while pure evolutionary algorithms are computationally prohibitive for online or high-dimensional settings. A robust, adaptive optimizer that dynamically leverages structure is needed for non-stationary network environments.
proposals.hypothesis: A two-layer optimizer, where an evolutionary metaheuristic adaptively configures the hyperparameters and sampling distribution of a concurrent SGD process, will converge faster and more robustly on large-scale structured matrix problems than standalone SGD or evolutionary search. This is because the evolutionary layer can efficiently discover and exploit structural patterns that are opaque to the gradient-based inner loop.
proposals.method: We propose a co-evolutionary framework where a Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) population operates on a meta-space of SGD configurations. Each particle encodes parameters like per-coordinate learning rates, momentum coefficients, and a parameterized importance sampling distribution for mini-batch selection. The fitness of a particle is the performance (e.g., validation loss) of its configured SGD instance after a short rollout on the structured matrix problem (e.g., a precoding optimization for a flexible metasurface). The PSO layer updates periodically, guiding the SGD's operational parameters to adapt to the evolving loss landscape.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel hybrid optimization algorithm specifically designed for high-dimensional, structured problems in communications and learning. It will provide a principled method for dynamic, structure-aware hyperparameter adaptation, reducing sensitivity to initial settings. The resulting optimizer will be validated on realistic problems like downlink sum-rate maximization with intelligent metasurfaces and large-scale direction-of-arrival estimation.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is using the evolutionary algorithm not to search the primary parameter space, but to meta-optimize the configuration and, crucially, the data-sampling strategy of a concurrent SGD solver. This explicit decoupling for structure discovery, particularly through adaptive importance sampling tailored to matrix structure, is a non-obvious fusion of population-based and gradient-based methods.

proposals.title: Stochastic Matrix-Monotonic Optimization for Large-Scale Network Resource Allocation
proposals.motivation: Matrix-monotonic optimization provides powerful, structure-aware solutions for problems like MIMO beamforming and network utility maximization, but its reliance on batch processing of full channel state information matrices limits scalability. Modern wireless networks, including those with intelligent metasurfaces, generate vast, streaming data, creating a critical need for online, large-scale optimization methods that can exploit problem geometry without prohibitive computational cost.
proposals.hypothesis: By embedding the inherent geodesic convexity and majorization structure of matrix-monotonic problems into stochastic first-order methods, we can develop algorithms that achieve optimal regret or convergence rates for very large-scale or online network optimization tasks, surpassing the performance of generic stochastic gradient descent.
proposals.method: We will develop a stochastic Riemannian optimization framework on the manifold of positive definite matrices, where the objective preserves matrix-monotonic properties. Specifically, we will design stochastic variants of the Wasserstein gradient flow and mirror descent, where the stochastic oracle provides unbiased estimates of gradient mappings derived from sampled user channels or network states. The core challenge is to maintain the majorization ordering in expectation, which we will address via a novel proximal stochastic update with a Bregman divergence derived from the matrix-monotonic function.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a new class of scalable, structure-preserving stochastic algorithms for communication and network optimization, directly applicable to massive MIMO and intelligent metasurface systems. It will provide theoretical guarantees on convergence and regret, bridging the gap between elegant matrix-analytic solutions and the demands of real-time, data-driven network operation.
proposals.novelty: While stochastic optimization and matrix-monotonic theory are mature fields, their integration for preserving problem-specific geodesic convexity in an online setting is unexplored. The novelty lies in the stochastic algorithmic design on matrix manifolds that explicitly respects the partial ordering (majorization) central to classical matrix-monotonic solutions.

proposals.title: Neuromorphic Predictive Coding for Adaptive CSI Feedback in FDD Massive MIMO
proposals.motivation: In FDD massive MIMO systems, the overhead of Channel State Information (CSI) feedback is a critical bottleneck for spectral efficiency. Existing compressed sensing and deep learning-based feedback schemes, like CsiNet, often treat feedback frames independently, failing to fully exploit the temporal correlation and structured sparsity inherent in real-world channel dynamics, leading to suboptimal compression.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing CSI feedback as a neuromorphic predictive coding problem, where a learned generative model at the UE predicts the channel and only sparse prediction errors are transmitted, will yield a more adaptive and fundamentally efficient feedback scheme that dynamically scales compression with channel coherence.
proposals.method: We will design a recurrent generative model (e.g., a Variational Recurrent Neural Network) at the UE to learn the spatio-temporal structure of the channel and predict future CSI. A sparsifying transform, optimized jointly with the model, will encode the prediction error. The gNB will use an identical model for reconstruction. The system will be trained end-to-end using a rate-distortion objective, balancing reconstruction error (NMSE) against feedback bit rate, and evaluated on standardized 3GPP channel models.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, biologically-inspired framework for adaptive CSI feedback that explicitly optimizes for temporal correlation. It will provide a new optimality benchmark for feedback overhead reduction in correlated channels and deliver a practical, learnable architecture that moves beyond static compression dictionaries.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the conceptual transfer of the predictive coding theory from computational neuroscience to wireless communications, creating a closed-loop, adaptive feedback system where the communicated quantity is a dynamically sparse innovation signal, not a compressed version of the raw channel state.

proposals.title: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Joint CSI Feedback Optimization and Resource Allocation in Underlay D2D Networks
proposals.motivation: In underlay D2D networks, efficient resource coexistence hinges on accurate yet low-overhead Channel State Information (CSI) feedback to manage cross-tier interference. Existing methods treat feedback design (compression, periodicity) and resource allocation as separate, static optimization problems, leading to suboptimal performance in dynamic environments. A co-design framework that adapts feedback characteristics to the instantaneous needs of the control policy is crucial for next-generation networks prioritizing spectral efficiency and low signaling overhead.
proposals.hypothesis: A Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) agent can learn to jointly optimize the CSI feedback strategy (including quantization, sparsity, and reporting frequency) and the transmission parameters (power, rate) for D2D pairs, achieving a superior trade-off between sum throughput and feedback overhead compared to decoupled, fixed-feedback schemes.
proposals.method: We propose a dual-timescale Markov Decision Process (MDP) where a central agent at the base station observes a state of partial network information. The agent's action space jointly defines the D2D power/rate allocations and a feedback policy (e.g., bit allocation for different channel dimensions, triggering condition for reports). We will employ a proximal policy optimization (PPO) or a deep deterministic policy gradient (DDPG) algorithm, depending on the action space discretization, trained in a simulated environment capturing realistic channel dynamics and interference. The reward function will combine weighted sum-rate and a penalty proportional to the feedback bits consumed.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel adaptive framework for integrated communication and control in wireless networks. It will provide concrete algorithms and performance benchmarks for DRL-based joint optimization, demonstrating significant gains in the throughput-overhead trade-off. The results will advance the theory of context-aware, goal-oriented communication for network optimization.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the closed-loop, learned co-design of the *content* and *frequency* of CSI feedback alongside the physical-layer resource allocation, moving beyond traditional separate compression and allocation stages. This treats feedback not as a fixed constraint but as a dynamic resource to be optimized by the DRL agent for its own decision-making efficacy.

proposals.title: Deep Unfolding for Fast and Robust Phase Optimization in Stacked Intelligent Metasurfaces
proposals.motivation: Stacked Intelligent Metasurfaces (SIMs) are a transformative technology for future wireless networks, enabling direct signal processing in the electromagnetic domain. However, optimizing the phase shifts across multiple metasurface layers for objectives like sum-rate maximization or DoA estimation is a high-dimensional, non-convex problem. Existing iterative optimization methods are computationally expensive and may converge slowly, hindering real-time application in dynamic environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a deep unfolding network, which unrolls the iterations of a gradient-based optimization algorithm into a trainable neural network architecture, can learn to optimize SIM phase shifts with significantly fewer computational steps while achieving superior or comparable performance to conventional solvers.
proposals.method: We will design a model-based deep unfolding network where each layer corresponds to one iteration of a projected gradient descent (PGD) algorithm for SIM phase optimization. The network's learnable parameters will include step sizes and regularization terms, which are adapted per layer. The architecture will incorporate the known physical model of the SIM's wave transformation as a fixed, differentiable computational block within each layer. We will train the network end-to-end using a loss function based on the wireless system objective (e.g., negative sum-rate) over a dataset of channel realizations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, efficient optimization framework for SIMs that bridges model-based optimization and data-driven learning. It will provide a substantial reduction in online computational latency for phase design, enabling more responsive SIM-aided communication and sensing systems. The resulting methodology will be a generalizable template for applying deep unfolding to other complex physical-layer optimization problems in beyond-5G networks.
proposals.novelty: While deep unfolding has been applied to traditional MIMO and IRS problems, its application to the multi-layer, cascaded channel model of a SIM is novel. The key innovation is the integration of the full, differentiable SIM physical model into the unfolded network layers, allowing the learning process to exploit and refine the structure of the underlying optimization steps specific to this hardware.

proposals.title: Robust Learning for Network Optimization via Diffusion Models with Suboptimal Data
proposals.motivation: Diffusion models for structured generation (DiffSG) show promise for solving complex network optimization problems by learning to generate high-quality solutions. However, their training relies on access to large datasets of optimal or near-optimal solutions, which are computationally prohibitive to obtain for many real-world, large-scale problems. This requirement severely limits the practical applicability of such learning-based solvers in wireless communications and network resource allocation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Diffusion model for Structured Generation (DiffSG) can be trained effectively on datasets composed of suboptimal, noisy, or even infeasible solution attempts, and that through a carefully designed denoising process and problem-aware loss functions, it can learn to generate solutions superior to its training data, approaching near-optimal performance.
proposals.method: We will adapt the DiffSG framework to accept solution sets from fast, heuristic algorithms (e.g., greedy methods) or perturbed optimal solutions as training data. The core technical innovation involves a two-stage training regime: first, a conditional diffusion model learns the manifold of feasible (but suboptimal) solutions; second, we integrate a problem-specific reward model (trained via contrastive learning on limited optimal examples) to guide the reverse diffusion process towards higher-quality outputs. This will be tested on canonical problems in Professor Poor's recent work, such as multiuser downlink beamforming and network resource allocation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for leveraging readily available, low-cost computational data to train high-performance generative solvers, dramatically reducing the data-generation bottleneck. It will advance the field of learning-aided network optimization by making data-driven methods viable for problems where optimal solvers are intractable, thus bridging the gap between heuristic and optimal performance.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses diffusion models to generate from optimal distributions, our approach deliberately learns from and improves upon suboptimal data, a paradigm shift for solver training. The integration of a learned reward critic within the diffusion sampling loop to 'denoise' solution quality, not just structure, is a novel methodological contribution.

proposals.title: Online Meta-Learning for Adaptive Network Optimization with Dynamic Topologies and Demands
proposals.motivation: Classical network optimization models assume static problem parameters, such as topology and traffic demand, during the solution process. This assumption is violated in modern wireless networks (e.g., with UAVs or reconfigurable intelligent surfaces) and dynamic computing clusters, where conditions evolve rapidly. Pre-2026 learning-based solvers, while promising, often fail when deployed in such non-stationary environments, leading to suboptimal or infeasible solutions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-learning framework, which continuously adapts a base optimization algorithm's parameters or representations based on a stream of observed problem changes, will outperform both static solvers and conventional online learning methods in dynamic network optimization tasks.
proposals.method: We propose an online meta-learning architecture where a recurrent neural network (RNN) or transformer meta-learner observes sequences of changes in network graph adjacency matrices and demand vectors. The meta-learner will output adaptive updates to the parameters of a graph neural network (GNN)-based solver core, which solves problems like power minimization or throughput maximization. This will be formulated as a bi-level online optimization, using techniques like online gradient-based meta-learning (e.g., Follow-The-Meta-Leader) to manage the non-stationarity. Performance will be benchmarked on simulated dynamic wireless networks and traffic engineering scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a principled framework for 'adaptive' problem-aware learning, where the solver itself evolves with the problem instance. It will provide new performance guarantees for online network optimization under structured non-stationarity. The resulting algorithms will be directly applicable to real-time control of intelligent metasurface-aided networks and elastic cloud infrastructures.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond learning a static mapping from problem parameters to solution, to learning an *adaptation policy* for the solver itself. This addresses a core limitation in pre-2026 learning-for-optimization research, which largely treats each problem instance as independent and identically distributed.

proposals.title: Learning to Optimize from Sparse Optimality: A Distributionally Robust Framework for Network Resource Allocation
proposals.motivation: Current learning-to-optimize (L2O) and optimization-inspired neural architectures often assume training data contains abundant, high-quality optimal or near-optimal solutions. In real-world network optimization (e.g., power control, user association), optimal solutions are computationally expensive to generate and may be extremely rare in a naturally occurring distribution of problem instances. This creates a critical gap between theory and practice, as models trained on suboptimal data may fail to generalize or discover efficient strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model can learn robust optimization policies even when optimal solutions are rare in the training distribution, by explicitly accounting for the distributional mismatch and leveraging structural priors from optimization theory.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage, distributionally robust framework. First, we employ a conditional generative model (e.g., a conditional Variational Autoencoder) to learn a latent representation of problem instances (e.g., channel states, user locations) from the observed, often suboptimal, solution distribution. Second, we train a solver network (e.g., a graph neural network for network problems) using a loss function that combines task performance with a distributionally robust penalty, such as a Wasserstein distance-based regularizer, to steer it towards the true optimal manifold despite sparse supervision. We will validate on downlink multiuser beamforming and network flow problems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled methodology for applying learning-based optimization in domains where optimal solutions are scarce. It will advance the field of problem-aware learning by formally addressing the data quality challenge. The resulting framework will be demonstrated to improve the sample efficiency and robustness of learned solvers for canonical wireless network optimization tasks.
proposals.novelty: The approach inverts the standard assumption that training data is rich in optimal solutions, focusing instead on learning from inherently suboptimal distributions. It uniquely combines generative modeling of problem-solution pairs with distributionally robust optimization to mitigate the bias in training data, a perspective not deeply explored in pre-2026 L2O literature.

proposals.title: Distributed Diffusion for Networked Data Generation: A Federated Optimization Framework
proposals.motivation: Diffusion models have become dominant for high-fidelity data generation, but their centralized training and sampling pose significant computational and privacy bottlenecks, especially for data inherently distributed across network nodes (e.g., in wireless sensing or federated datasets). Pre-2026, federated learning has been applied to model training, but the iterative, sequential nature of the diffusion sampling process remains a centralized computational challenge, limiting scalability and real-time application in resource-constrained networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the diffusion sampling process can be decomposed and distributed across a network via a problem-aware, federated optimization framework, achieving near-optimal sample quality with substantial reductions in latency and communication overhead compared to centralized sampling.
proposals.method: We will formulate distributed diffusion sampling as a consensus optimization problem, where each network node is responsible for a subset of timesteps in the reverse diffusion chain. We will leverage the pre-2026 concept of stacked intelligent metasurfaces as a model for structured, multi-layer computation, designing a message-passing algorithm where nodes iteratively refine their local latent variables. The core technical approach involves solving a constrained matrix completion problem at each iteration to ensure global consistency of the generated sample, using accelerated gradient methods to achieve optimal convergence rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, principled framework for distributed generative inference, extending federated paradigms beyond training to the sampling phase. It will provide new theoretical insights into the decomposability of diffusion processes and deliver practical algorithms for scalable, privacy-preserving data synthesis in edge networks.
proposals.novelty: While federated training is well-studied, distributing the sequential sampling process of a diffusion model is a non-trivial, unexplored problem. The novelty lies in treating sampling as a networked consensus optimization, marrying diffusion models with real-time network control algorithms from pre-2026 wireless communications research.

proposals.title: Generalizing Differentiable Surrogate Gradients for Scalable Optimization in Wireless Network Design
proposals.motivation: Network optimization problems, such as joint beamforming and resource allocation in multiuser MIMO systems, are often NP-hard and rely on iterative solvers that are computationally expensive and lack end-to-end differentiability. Pre-2026, the DiffSG framework demonstrated promise for specific problems by embedding surrogate gradients of classical algorithms into deep learning pipelines. However, its applicability to a broader class of network optimization problems with complex, coupled constraints remains unexplored, limiting its impact on real-time network control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the DiffSG framework can be systematically generalized to solve a wider class of constrained network optimization problems by developing problem-aware surrogate gradient modules for key subroutines like fractional programming and interference management, leading to faster convergence and near-optimal solutions compared to traditional optimization methods.
proposals.method: We will extend DiffSG by first formulating two new target problems: (1) energy-efficient power control in cell-free massive MIMO networks and (2) dynamic user association and bandwidth slicing in Open RAN. For each, we will design differentiable surrogate models for critical, non-differentiable operations (e.g., weighted minimum mean square error (WMMSE) updates, branch-and-bound decisions) using continuous relaxations and stochastic smoothing. These modules will be integrated into a meta-learning architecture that learns to warm-start and guide the optimization process, with loss functions combining task performance and constraint satisfaction penalties.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, extensible toolkit for applying differentiable programming to complex network optimization, moving beyond proof-of-concept. It will deliver new, efficient algorithms for the selected wireless problems, accompanied by theoretical analysis on convergence conditions. The resulting framework will demonstrate how learning can be tightly coupled with model-based optimization for improved scalability.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic creation of differentiable surrogates for core network optimization primitives beyond initial demonstrations, and in the co-design of a learning architecture that adapts to multiple problem structures, rather than being tailored to a single task.

proposals.title: Constrained Diffusion Models for Network Optimization: A Problem-Aware Generative Framework
proposals.motivation: Diffusion models have shown remarkable success in generative tasks, but their application to structured engineering problems like network optimization remains limited. Current approaches often treat the optimization variable as a generic data sample, ignoring critical domain constraints (e.g., power budgets, interference thresholds, connectivity), leading to infeasible or suboptimal solutions. Integrating this prior knowledge directly into the generative process is essential for producing valid, high-performance network configurations efficiently.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating hard network constraints directly into the reverse diffusion process via projection or guided sampling will yield a generative model that produces provably feasible and near-optimal solutions for complex network optimization problems, outperforming both generic learning-based solvers and traditional iterative algorithms in sample efficiency.
proposals.method: We will develop a problem-aware diffusion model where the denoising network is trained to predict clean network parameters (e.g., beamforming matrices, link schedules) from noisy versions. Crucially, during the reverse diffusion sampling, we will interleave each denoising step with a projection operation onto the feasible set defined by the problem's convex or non-convex constraints (e.g., using proximal operators or manifold projections). For probabilistic constraints, we will employ classifier-free guidance conditioned on constraint satisfaction metrics. The framework will be validated on two canonical problems from the researcher's portfolio: multiuser MIMO beamforming for sum-rate maximization and sensor network topology optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for embedding domain knowledge into generative models for engineering design, moving beyond simple post-processing. It will provide a practical, learning-based tool for solving constrained network optimization problems with guarantees on solution feasibility. The resulting framework will bridge the gap between data-driven generative AI and classical model-based optimization in communications and networking.
proposals.novelty: While diffusion models have been guided by classifiers or simple conditioning, the explicit, iterative enforcement of hard engineering constraints within the generative sampling loop is novel. This approach fundamentally departs from treating constraints as mere components of a loss function, instead ensuring strict adherence throughout the solution generation process.

proposals.title: A Unified Benchmarking Framework for Network Optimization: Evaluating the Efficacy of Problem-Aware Learning Against Classical and Modern Solvers
proposals.motivation: Recent advances in problem-aware learning (PAL) for network optimization, such as those proposed for cross-problem solving, promise to combine the speed of data-driven methods with the interpretability of model-based optimization. However, their true performance envelope remains unclear due to ad-hoc comparisons, often limited to generic deep learning baselines or a single classical solver. A systematic, large-scale evaluation against a comprehensive suite of state-of-the-art solvers is urgently needed to establish the practical value and limitations of the PAL paradigm in pre-2026 research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that problem-aware learning architectures, when rigorously benchmarked, will demonstrate a consistent Pareto dominance—superior trade-offs between computational speed, solution quality, and generalization—over both traditional optimization methods (e.g., convex solvers, metaheuristics) and black-box learning-based approaches across a spectrum of canonical network problems.
proposals.method: We will design a modular benchmarking framework that evaluates PAL models (e.g., graph neural networks with embedded optimization layers) against: 1) Traditional solvers (CVX, Gurobi, ADMM for convex problems; simulated annealing for non-convex), 2) Generic deep learning baselines (fully connected and convolutional networks), and 3) Recent specialized learning-for-optimization methods (e.g., differentiable optimization layers, reinforcement learning agents). The test suite will include problems from the researcher's recent work, such as multiuser beamforming and DoA estimation, plus standard problems like max-flow and facility location. Metrics will include optimality gap, inference time, training sample efficiency, and robustness to distribution shift.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous, multi-dimensional performance map for the emerging PAL paradigm, identifying its strengths and failure modes. It will yield a public benchmark suite to guide future algorithm development and deliver clear, evidence-based guidelines for when to deploy PAL versus established methods in network engineering.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in a new algorithm, but in the comprehensive, problem-aware benchmarking methodology itself, which systematically dissects the trade-offs of hybrid learning-optimization methods against the full spectrum of pre-2026 solvers, moving beyond isolated proof-of-concept demonstrations.

proposals.title: Latent Diffusion for Efficient Network Optimization: Compressing Solution Manifolds in Wireless Systems
proposals.motivation: Optimal network solutions (e.g., beamforming matrices, power allocations) for complex wireless systems like those using Intelligent Metasurfaces (IM) are high-dimensional and computationally expensive to generate. Real-time adaptation requires solving these optimization problems repeatedly under varying channel conditions, creating a prohibitive computational burden. A method to efficiently generate high-quality, near-optimal solutions from a compressed representation is urgently needed for practical deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the manifold of near-optimal solutions for parametric network optimization problems can be learned and efficiently sampled in a low-dimensional latent space using a diffusion model, enabling rapid generation of high-performance solutions with minimal computational overhead.
proposals.method: We will train a variational autoencoder (VAE) to compress high-dimensional solution matrices (e.g., precoding matrices for downlink multi-user MIMO with IM) into a structured latent space. A denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM) will then be trained on this latent space to learn the distribution of latent vectors corresponding to near-optimal solutions for a range of channel parameters. For inference, given new channel state information, a lightweight optimizer will find a point in the latent space via the diffusion process, and the decoder will generate the full solution matrix.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for amortized optimization in networks, drastically reducing online computation. It will provide theoretical and empirical analysis of the trade-off between latent space compression, solution optimality, and generation speed. The method will be validated on canonical problems in wireless communications, such as beamforming for stacked intelligent metasurfaces.
proposals.novelty: While diffusion models are established for image generation, their application to learn and sample from the solution manifold of parametric network optimization problems is novel. This approach moves beyond standard supervised learning or reinforcement learning by explicitly modeling the distribution of optimal solutions in a compressed, generative framework.

proposals.title: Flow Matching for Fast, Scalable Optimization in Wireless Networks
proposals.motivation: Recent work on generative AI for network optimization, particularly using diffusion models, shows promise for solving complex, high-dimensional problems like resource allocation and beamforming. However, diffusion models are notoriously slow to train and sample from due to their iterative denoising process. This computational bottleneck limits their real-time application in dynamic wireless environments, a core interest in Prof. Poor's research on intelligent metasurfaces and multiuser communications.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing the diffusion process with Continuous Normalizing Flows (CNFs) trained via Flow Matching will enable the generation of high-quality solutions to network optimization problems with significantly faster training convergence and sampling speed, while maintaining or improving solution optimality.
proposals.method: We will develop a problem-aware generative model where the target distribution is defined by optimal or near-optimal solutions to specific network optimization tasks (e.g., power allocation, DoA estimation). The model will be a neural network parameterizing a time-dependent vector field, trained with the simulation-free Flow Matching objective, which provides a more direct and stable training path than score matching. We will adapt the architecture to respect problem structure, potentially using graph neural networks for network topology and incorporating constraints directly into the flow. Inference will involve a single, fast ODE solve from a simple prior to the complex solution distribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, efficient generative framework for solving families of network optimization problems, directly contributing to the field of AI-for-networks. It will yield a practical method with demonstrably faster training and inference times than diffusion-based approaches, facilitating real-time adaptive optimization. The research will also explore the trade-offs between speed, solution quality, and constraint satisfaction in this new paradigm.
proposals.novelty: While Flow Matching is emerging in machine learning, its application to structured engineering optimization problems, particularly in wireless communications and networking, is novel. The key innovation is the integration of domain-specific problem structure and constraints into a fast, flow-based generative model, moving beyond slower, generic diffusion frameworks.

proposals.title: Generative Optimization for Wireless Networks: A Transformer-Guided Diffusion Framework
proposals.motivation: Optimal resource allocation in dense, dynamic wireless networks (e.g., for multiuser MIMO or intelligent metasurface configuration) involves solving high-dimensional, non-convex problems with complex structural dependencies. Traditional optimization methods are often too slow for real-time operation, while standard learning approaches struggle to generalize across diverse network states and problem instances. A generative framework that can produce high-quality, feasible solutions by learning the intricate structure of optimal solution spaces is critically needed.
proposals.hypothesis: A diffusion-based generative model, with a transformer architecture as its denoising network, can effectively capture the long-range dependencies and permutation invariances inherent in network optimization problems, thereby generating near-optimal solutions faster and with better generalization than problem-specific solvers or other neural network approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a conditional denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM) where the reverse process is parameterized by a transformer network. The model will be trained on datasets of optimal (or high-quality) solutions paired with problem parameters (e.g., channel state information, user locations). The transformer's self-attention mechanism will explicitly model interactions between all network elements (users, antennas, metasurface elements). We will integrate problem-specific constraints (e.g., power limits, interference thresholds) into the loss function and the diffusion sampling process to ensure feasibility.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel generative paradigm for network optimization, providing a fast, amortized solver for a class of structured problems. It will advance the integration of deep generative models with communication theory, specifically demonstrating how transformer-based diffusion models can learn and exploit the underlying geometry of optimal solution sets. The resulting framework will be validated on downlink beamforming and intelligent metasurface configuration problems, showing superior sample efficiency and generalization compared to GANs or pure reinforcement learning methods.
proposals.novelty: While diffusion models have been applied to image generation, their application to structured network optimization problems is nascent. The key novelty is the deliberate use of a transformer as the denoising network to capture the relational graph structure of network problems, moving beyond convolutional U-Nets, which are less suited for non-Euclidean network data. This merges the strengths of transformers for structured data with the stable training and mode coverage of diffusion models.

proposals.title: Monte Carlo Tree Search-Guided Diffusion for High-Dimensional Network Optimization
proposals.motivation: High-dimensional, non-convex optimization problems in wireless network design, such as beamforming and resource allocation, remain computationally challenging. While diffusion models have shown promise as powerful generative samplers for solution spaces, their standard denoising process lacks strategic exploration, often converging to suboptimal modes. Integrating principled planning could significantly enhance the quality and efficiency of discovered solutions.
proposals.hypothesis: Guiding the reverse diffusion process with a Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) planner will enable more intelligent exploration of the solution space, leading to the discovery of higher-quality, near-optimal configurations for complex network optimization problems compared to standalone diffusion or traditional solvers.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel MCTS-Diffusion framework. A conditional diffusion model, trained on historical problem instances and solutions, will serve as a learnable prior and policy network for MCTS. At each step of the iterative denoising (reverse diffusion) trajectory, MCTS will be employed to plan several steps ahead, evaluating potential future states (partial solutions) using a surrogate value network. The search will bias the sampling process towards promising regions of the solution space, effectively interleaving generative sampling with strategic planning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new hybrid algorithm that merges generative AI with classical planning for optimization. It will provide a rigorous benchmark against gradient-based methods and pure learning-based solvers in problems like intelligent metasurface configuration and multiuser beamforming. The proposed method aims to advance the field of problem-aware learning by making the solution search process more deliberate and sample-efficient.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the tight integration of MCTS as an *inner-loop* planner within the diffusion sampling process, rather than using them as separate, sequential components. This moves beyond using diffusion models merely as black-box samplers and instead uses planning to actively steer the generative denoising trajectory, a non-obvious synthesis given the predominant use of diffusion in unimodal data generation.

proposals.title: Distilled Diffusion for Edge Optimization: A Problem-Aware Learning Framework for Real-Time Network Control
proposals.motivation: The deployment of intelligent surfaces and dense edge networks requires real-time, adaptive optimization algorithms for tasks like beamforming and resource allocation. However, classical iterative solvers and large neural models are computationally prohibitive for resource-constrained edge devices. There is a critical need for ultra-lightweight, high-performance models that can perform inference directly on-device to enable low-latency network control.
proposals.hypothesis: A problem-aware knowledge distillation framework, which transfers the iterative refinement capability of a pre-trained diffusion model into a tiny student network, can achieve near-optimal solution quality for parametric network optimization problems while meeting the strict latency and memory constraints of edge hardware.
proposals.method: We will first train a teacher diffusion model, formulated as a stochastic sampler for solutions to parametric optimization problems (e.g., weighted sum-rate maximization). We will then design a compact student architecture, leveraging techniques like depthwise separable convolutions and attention condensation. The core of our approach is a novel distillation loss that aligns not only the output solutions but also the intermediate denoising trajectories of the teacher, enforcing the student to learn the underlying optimization dynamics. Training will use a dataset of problem instances and solutions generated via the teacher and conventional solvers.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, deployable framework for lightweight on-device optimization, directly addressing the compute-memory bottleneck in edge AI. It will advance the theory of problem-aware learning by showing how diffusion models' generative process can be distilled. The resulting tiny models will be validated on real-world edge tasks such as reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) configuration and multiuser link adaptation.
proposals.novelty: While model compression exists, distilling the *iterative generative process* of a diffusion model, specifically for solving parametric optimization problems, is unexplored. This moves beyond compressing discriminative models and tailors the distillation to the structure of network optimization tasks, merging insights from generative AI and mathematical optimization.

proposals.title: Inverse Network Design via Differentiable Surrogate Gradients: Inferring Optimal Parameters from Performance Profiles
proposals.motivation: Network design traditionally follows a forward process: given parameters (e.g., link capacities, beamforming weights), performance is analyzed. The inverse problem—finding parameters that achieve a desired performance profile (e.g., latency, throughput targets)—is crucial for automated network orchestration but is analytically intractable and often under-constrained. Pre-2026, deep learning has shown promise for forward network modeling, but a robust, differentiable framework for the inverse design of complex communication and sensor networks is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: A differentiable surrogate gradient (DiffSG) framework, which learns a smooth mapping from network performance metrics back to physical or protocol parameters, can efficiently and accurately solve the ill-posed inverse network design problem for wireless systems.
proposals.method: We will develop a DiffSG architecture where a neural network surrogate models the forward performance of a network (e.g., SINR, DoA estimation error) as a differentiable function of tunable parameters (e.g., RIS phase shifts, power allocations). This surrogate will be trained on data generated from established models (e.g., MIMO channel models, array processing). The inverse design will then be formulated as a constrained optimization problem, solved via gradient descent through the surrogate, using implicit differentiation or adjoint methods to backpropagate from the loss on desired performance to the input parameter space. We will validate on case studies: reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) configuration for multiuser downlink and stacked metasurface design for 2D direction-of-arrival estimation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a general, learning-based methodology for inverse network design, moving beyond trial-and-error or exhaustive search. It will yield specific algorithms for inferring optimal RIS and sensor array parameters from high-level performance specifications, directly applicable to 6G and integrated sensing and communications (ISAC) systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses learning for forward prediction or direct policy optimization, our approach explicitly constructs a differentiable pathway for inverting the network design process, enabling gradient-based inference of optimal physical-layer parameters from abstract performance goals, a non-obvious extension of differentiable programming to network engineering.

proposals.title: Analyzing Solution Manifolds: A Symbolic Benchmark for Differentiable Symbolic Graph Learning
proposals.motivation: Differentiable Symbolic Graph (DiffSG) methods promise to learn solution distributions for complex network optimization problems, but their behavior on fundamental, interpretable problems is poorly understood. Grounding analysis in small-scale, symbolic problems (e.g., classic combinatorial or polynomial root-finding tasks) is crucial for pre-2026 research to establish theoretical baselines and interpretability before scaling to large-scale, black-box network applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that on low-dimensional symbolic problems, DiffSG will learn structured solution manifolds that reveal its inductive biases, and that its performance will correlate with the algebraic complexity of the underlying problem, providing a predictive model for its scalability.
proposals.method: We will construct a benchmark of few-variable symbolic problems (e.g., solving systems of low-degree polynomials, small Max-Cut, traveling salesman problems). We will implement a DiffSG architecture where nodes represent operations and edges represent differentiable path weights, trained via gradient descent on a loss combining solution feasibility and diversity. Analysis will employ tools from optimal transport and matrix analysis of the learned weight distributions to characterize the solution manifold geometry.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first principled, interpretable benchmark for evaluating DiffSG's solution distribution learning, linking its performance to problem structure. It will yield concrete design guidelines for applying DiffSG to larger-scale network optimization and signal processing problems, such as those in intelligent metasurface configuration.
proposals.novelty: While DiffSG is applied to large problems, a systematic, symbolic-scale analysis of its learned distributions is absent. This proposal uniquely bridges symbolic computation and differentiable learning to deconstruct the 'black box' of problem-aware learning.

proposals.title: Generating Adversarial Benchmarks for Network Optimization via Learned Instance Distributions
proposals.motivation: The robustness and generalization of network optimization solvers are critically assessed through benchmark testing. However, standard benchmarks often fail to expose pathological weaknesses, as they may not represent the most challenging instances within a problem's feasible space. Pre-2026 advances in learning-augmented optimization provide a unique opportunity to systematically probe solver vulnerabilities, moving beyond random or historical instance generation.
proposals.hypothesis: A generative model, trained on a learned distribution of problem instances from a specific class (e.g., wireless scheduling, DoA estimation), can be guided to synthesize novel, high-difficulty instances that serve as adversarial benchmarks, exposing failure modes and performance limits of state-of-the-art solvers.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage framework. First, a conditional variational autoencoder (CVAE) or a normalizing flow will be trained to model the distribution of problem parameters (e.g., channel matrices, user geometries) from historical or simulated data. Second, we will employ a gradient-based adversarial search or a reinforcement learning agent within this latent space to maximize a difficulty metric, such as solver runtime, constraint violation likelihood, or optimality gap. This process will be tailored to network problems central to Poor's recent work, such as multiuser downlink optimization with intelligent metasurface constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a principled methodology for creating stress-test benchmarks for network optimization algorithms. It will provide new insights into the structural properties that make instances hard for specific solver classes (e.g., convex approximations, metaheuristics). The resulting benchmark suite will enable more rigorous validation and guide the development of more robust optimization methods.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial example generation is known in deep learning, its application to create hard instances for classical network optimization solvers via a learned generative model is novel. This approach moves beyond simple parameter perturbation to explore the structured, high-dimensional feasible space of communications and estimation problems.

proposals.title: Minimax Resource Provisioning for Guaranteed QoS: Stochastic Control of Peak Power and Harvested Energy
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of energy-harvesting IoT devices and power-constrained edge systems necessitates a paradigm shift from average-case to worst-case resource design. Existing power control policies optimize average performance under average power constraints, which can lead to service outages during periods of resource scarcity. This work addresses the critical need to provision the minimal physical resource infrastructure—such as peak power draw or battery capacity—required to guarantee a hard quality-of-service (QoS) target, ensuring reliable operation in volatile environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formulating and solving stochastic minimax optimization problems with hard QoS constraints, we can derive novel control policies that explicitly trade off performance risk against resource volatility, thereby enabling the design of systems with provably minimal resource provisioning for guaranteed reliability.
proposals.method: We will formulate the problem as a constrained stochastic optimization where the objective is to minimize the essential supremum (peak) of the power process or the integral of a harvesting process, subject to a probabilistic or almost-sure constraint on a performance metric like data rate. Leveraging Professor Poor's expertise in optimization and learning, we will develop solution methods combining Lyapunov optimization techniques for time-average constraints with risk-aware reinforcement learning (e.g., distributional RL or constrained policy optimization) to handle extremal metrics. The analysis will employ tools from extreme value theory and stochastic network calculus to characterize the required resource provisioning.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a new theoretical framework for minimax resource provisioning in stochastic networks. It will yield practically implementable control algorithms that minimize capital expenditure on power systems and energy buffers while guaranteeing QoS. The work will provide fundamental performance bounds linking resource volatility, channel uncertainty, and guaranteed service levels.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in shifting the optimization objective from an expectation to a temporal extremum or integral under a hard performance constraint, a largely unexplored direction in stochastic network optimization. This moves beyond traditional ergodic capacity and average power analysis to directly address infrastructure sizing and performance risk.

proposals.title: Predictive Resource Allocation via Mobility-Aware Manifold Optimization for Dynamic MIMO Networks
proposals.motivation: The performance of dense wireless networks, especially those employing massive MIMO and intelligent surfaces, is critically limited by dynamic interference from user mobility. Traditional reactive resource allocation causes latency and instability. A predictive framework that leverages the inherent smoothness of user trajectories to anticipate channel state evolution is essential for next-generation network efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: Modeling the temporal evolution of channel states, conditioned on predicted user mobility, as a smooth trajectory on a Riemannian matrix manifold (e.g., the fixed-rank positive semidefinite manifold) will enable the proactive solution of network optimization problems, yielding resource allocation policies with superior spectral efficiency and lower latency compared to myopic methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, a graph neural network (GNN) will model the network topology and predict short-term user mobility, generating a sequence of expected channel covariance matrices. Second, we will formulate the predictive beamforming and power allocation problem as an optimization over the curve connecting these matrices on the Riemannian manifold of fixed-rank PSD matrices. We will solve this using manifold optimization techniques like Riemannian conjugate gradient or trust-region methods, ensuring solutions respect the inherent rank and structure constraints of the multi-user MIMO system.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, geometrically grounded algorithm for predictive resource allocation that is both computationally efficient and structure-preserving. It will deliver a significant performance benchmark for dynamic network optimization and offer a new methodology fusing predictive learning with constrained optimization on manifolds, applicable beyond communications to other network control problems.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of a learned mobility predictor with Riemannian optimization on the channel state trajectory is novel. While manifold optimization for static MIMO problems exists, its application to a predicted, smooth evolution of the optimization domain for proactive control is unexplored and non-obvious.

proposals.title: Decentralized D2D Power and Rate Control via Multi-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning with a Centralized Interference-Aware Critic
proposals.motivation: In underlay Device-to-Device (D2D) communication, densely deployed links must autonomously manage transmit power and data rates to maximize network capacity. This is a complex, non-convex optimization problem due to coupled interference constraints and the need for real-time adaptation to dynamic channel states. Traditional optimization methods often struggle with scalability and require excessive signaling overhead.
proposals.hypothesis: A Centralized Training with Decentralized Execution (CTDE) framework, using a Multi-Agent Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (MADDPG) algorithm, can enable D2D links to learn decentralized, cooperative policies that implicitly coordinate to maximize sum spectral efficiency while strictly adhering to dynamic interference constraints imposed on primary cellular users.
proposals.method: We formulate the D2D network as a Markov game where each link is an agent. Each agent's policy network (actor) maps local observations (e.g., its own channel gain) to a continuous action (power, rate). A centralized critic network, accessible only during training, is fed the global state (all channel states, aggregate interference at primary receivers) and all agents' actions to compute a Q-value. The critic guides the training of all decentralized actors via the MADDPG policy gradient. The reward function will balance sum-rate maximization with a penalty for violating primary user interference temperature limits.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, model-free solution for distributed interference management in D2D underlays, reducing the need for centralized computation and frequent global channel information exchange during execution. It will demonstrate how CTDE frameworks, successful in other domains, can be adapted to solve core wireless resource allocation problems with hard constraints.
proposals.novelty: The adaptation of the CTDE/MADDPG framework, prominent in multi-agent robotics and traffic control, to the specific domain of D2D underlay communications with its unique interference coupling and hard constraint dynamics is novel. This approach moves beyond single-agent or fully centralized RL solutions prevalent in pre-2026 wireless literature.

proposals.title: Robust Deep Reinforcement Learning for Joint Power and Rate Control in D2D Underlay Networks with Imperfect Channel Statistics
proposals.motivation: Device-to-device (D2D) underlay networks enhance spectral efficiency but create complex interference management problems. Existing DRL solutions for dynamic resource allocation often assume perfect knowledge of channel distribution information (CDI), leading to severe performance degradation when faced with model errors or distributional shifts. A principled framework that combines the adaptability of DRL with the reliability of robust optimization is critically needed for practical deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a distributionally robust optimization (DRO) objective, defined over a Wasserstein or moment-based uncertainty set, into a DRL agent's learning process will yield a policy that maintains high performance under the worst-case statistical deviations, thereby outperforming standard DRL and conventional robust optimization in dynamic environments with imperfect CDI.
proposals.method: We will formulate a robust Markov Decision Process (MDP) where the reward function incorporates a DRO cost. The agent, based on a Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) or Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) architecture, will be trained with a two-timescale approach: the inner loop solves for the worst-case distribution within the current uncertainty set, while the outer loop updates the policy network parameters. The state representation will be augmented with features characterizing the estimated CDI and its confidence bounds to inform the robust learning process.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, unified algorithmic framework for robust real-time resource allocation in wireless networks. It will advance the theoretical understanding of robust DRL by formally bridging DRO and sequential decision-making. The resulting solution will offer demonstrable resilience to channel model inaccuracies, a key step towards data-driven control in practical wireless systems.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the deep integration of a DRO objective directly into the DRL agent's learning loop, rather than applying robust optimization as a pre-processing step or using DRL as a mere solver for a static robust problem. This enables the policy to adaptively balance performance and robustness based on real-time network state and uncertainty.

proposals.title: Adaptive Semantic Compression for Knowledge Graphs via Problem-Aware Graph Neural Networks
proposals.motivation: The efficient transmission and storage of knowledge graphs (KGs) is critical for large-scale networked AI systems, from semantic networks to distributed databases. Traditional compression methods are agnostic to the underlying graph semantics and the specific downstream tasks (e.g., link prediction, node classification), leading to suboptimal trade-offs between compression rate and task performance. An adaptive, semantic-aware compression strategy is needed to optimize network resource utilization while preserving application-specific utility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Graph Neural Network (GNN) can learn to perform adaptive, lossy compression of knowledge graphs by optimizing a rate-distortion trade-off where the 'distortion' is defined by the degradation in performance on a set of downstream inference tasks, and that this problem-aware approach will outperform task-agnostic compression baselines.
proposals.method: We will formulate semantic compression as a joint optimization problem over a GNN-based encoder, a bottleneck, and a decoder. The encoder, built upon a graph attention network (GAT), will learn node and edge embeddings. A sparsification or quantization module will impose the compression. The decoder will be a task-specific GNN (e.g., for link prediction). We will train the system end-to-end using a Lagrangian loss function, L = Rate + λ * Task_Loss, adapting methods from neural compression and leveraging gradient-based optimization to learn the compression strategy. The framework will be evaluated on standard KG benchmarks like FB15k-237.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, learnable framework for task-aware compression of structured data, directly addressing the network optimization problem of efficient KG distribution. It will provide new theoretical insights into the rate-distortion-performance trade-off for relational data and deliver a practical algorithm that can be integrated into knowledge-driven communication systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of downstream task performance as the distortion metric within a GNN-based compression loop, moving beyond signal reconstruction fidelity to semantic utility, and in applying a 'problem-aware learning' philosophy—a theme from the researcher's recent work—to the domain of knowledge graph compression.

proposals.title: Energy-Minimal Semantic-Aware Beamforming via Cross-Problem Learning and Intelligent Metasurface Optimization
proposals.motivation: The shift from bit-based to semantic-aware communications, as defined by pre-2026 semantic information theory, promises massive efficiency gains but introduces a critical resource allocation challenge. Existing work focuses on maximizing semantic rate; its dual—minimizing total system energy (transmit power and RIS control) under per-user semantic rate constraints—is essential for sustainable 6G networks and IoT but remains unsolved.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a problem-aware learning framework, integrating semantic entropy models with physical-layer constraints, can efficiently solve this non-convex energy-minimization problem, yielding a Pareto-optimal trade-off between semantic performance and energy consumption.
proposals.method: We will formulate the problem as a joint optimization of transmit beamforming matrices and the phase-shift matrix of a flexible intelligent metasurface (FIM). The core is a novel two-stage algorithm: first, a transformer-based model learns to map semantic rate constraints and channel states to a near-optimal initialization by leveraging cross-problem learning from related network optimization tasks. Second, we employ a matrix-based alternating optimization scheme, using successive convex approximation and Riemannian manifold optimization for the FIM, to converge to a locally optimal solution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic framework for energy-minimal semantic-aware resource allocation. It will deliver a practical algorithm for RIS-aided semantic networks and generate fundamental insights into the energy-semantics Pareto frontier, directly informing green 6G system design.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in solving the dual energy-minimization problem for semantic communications and in the fusion of cross-problem learning with FIM optimization, moving beyond traditional bit-based or heuristic semantic approaches.

proposals.title: Optimizing Federated Edge Learning via a Physics-Sensing-Communication Network Backbone
proposals.motivation: Federated edge learning (FEL) is constrained by the communication-computation trade-off and heterogeneous network conditions at the wireless edge. The emerging paradigm of physics-sensing-communication (PSC) networks, which co-design sensing, communication, and physical layer control, offers a new substrate for resource optimization. This work investigates whether a PSC backbone can fundamentally improve the efficiency and convergence of FEL by exploiting its inherent cross-layer awareness and resource adaptability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling the FEL process as a network utility maximization problem over a PSC graph will yield novel scheduling and resource allocation policies that significantly reduce total training time and energy consumption compared to conventional wireless backbones.
proposals.method: We will formulate a joint optimization problem for client selection, model aggregation, and physical resource (e.g., spectrum, metasurface phase shifts, sensing slots) allocation. The PSC backbone will be modeled as a dynamic graph where edges represent reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS)-assisted links with integrated sensing capabilities. We will develop a distributed, problem-aware learning algorithm, likely based on primal-dual or Lyapunov optimization methods, to solve this stochastic optimization. Performance will be evaluated via simulation using standard FEL benchmarks and realistic channel models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous framework for FEL over next-generation PSC networks, delivering new optimal scheduling algorithms. It will quantify the gains in convergence rate and resource efficiency achievable through integrated sensing and communication for machine learning. The results will bridge the gap between physical-layer network control and distributed AI.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes FEL over static or conventional wireless models, the novelty lies in explicitly leveraging the reconfigurable, sensing-augmented physical layer of a PSC network as a tunable parameter for learning orchestration, creating a tightly coupled cross-layer control loop.

proposals.title: Structured Embeddings for Cross-Domain Network Optimization: A Graph-Attention and Symbolic Encoding Approach
proposals.motivation: Recent work on problem-aware learning for network optimization shows promise, but its success hinges on how the underlying mathematical problem (e.g., a power allocation or DoA estimation formulation) is encoded for a neural network. Current methods often rely on naive tokenization of equations or flattened constraint matrices, which may lose critical structural and relational information, limiting generalization across problem families. Developing more sophisticated, structure-preserving encodings is a key bottleneck for advancing learning-based solvers in communications and signal processing.
proposals.hypothesis: Encoding mathematical optimization problems using a hybrid of graph-based representations (capturing variable-constraint relationships) and symbolic embeddings (capturing operator semantics) will yield more expressive and transferable latent representations than current sequence-based methods, leading to improved performance in cross-problem learning for network optimization tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel encoding framework where a problem's objective and constraints are parsed into a bipartite graph connecting variable and constraint nodes, annotated with operator and coefficient features. This graph will be processed by a Graph Attention Network (GAT) to generate a structured embedding. Concurrently, symbolic tokens (e.g., log, max, ||.||) will be passed through a dedicated embedding layer. These two embeddings will be fused and used as input to a transformer-based meta-learner, such as a Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) setup, trained on a corpus of related network problems (e.g., power control, beamforming, DoA estimation). Performance will be benchmarked against sequence-encoded baselines on few-shot adaptation and optimality gap metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled methodology for representing mathematical optimization problems as structured data for machine learning, moving beyond simple sequence models. It will deliver a concrete, open-source framework for problem encoding and demonstrate its superiority in cross-problem generalization within communication network optimization, a core interest of the researcher. The results will directly inform the design of more robust and sample-efficient learning-based solvers.
proposals.novelty: While GATs and symbolic embeddings exist separately, their combined application to tokenize entire *mathematical problem formulations* for meta-learning in networking is novel. This approach explicitly models the problem's syntactic structure and semantic content, which is a significant departure from treating the problem description as a flat sequence of tokens.

proposals.title: A Meta-Learning Framework for General-Purpose Optimization in Dynamic Networks
proposals.motivation: Modern networks (wireless, IoT, sensor) face diverse, dynamic optimization tasks like resource allocation, beamforming, and routing. Current solvers are often bespoke, requiring extensive re-engineering for each new problem, which is inefficient and lacks adaptability. A universal solver framework could dramatically accelerate the deployment and management of intelligent networks, a critical need in the pre-2026 era of burgeoning complexity and scale.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learning architecture, trained on a diverse corpus of network optimization problems, can learn a shared representation and adaptation strategy to rapidly generate near-optimal solutions for unseen but related network management tasks.
proposals.method: We propose a hybrid model combining a graph neural network (GNN) encoder to handle arbitrary network topologies with a hypernetwork meta-learner. The GNN extracts task-specific features, while the hypernetwork, conditioned on these features and a formal problem description, generates the parameters for a lightweight, task-specific solver (e.g., a small optimization network or a learned gradient descent rule). Training will use a curriculum on a synthetic dataset spanning convex, combinatorial, and non-convex network problems (e.g., max-flow, power control, link scheduling).
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a flexible, proof-of-concept 'solver generator' for network operations, reducing the need for custom algorithm design. It will provide theoretical and empirical insights into the transferability of structural knowledge across network optimization problems, advancing the field of learning-augmented optimization.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been applied to narrow problem classes, its integration with GNNs and hypernetworks to create a unified generator for *diverse* network optimization tasks—from physical-layer beamforming to network-layer routing—is a novel and ambitious synthesis beyond current problem-aware learning approaches.

proposals.title: Learning to Solve: Graph Neural Networks for Unifying Constraint and Variable Structures in Network Optimization
proposals.motivation: Many core problems in wireless networks, such as power allocation, beamforming, and scheduling, are NP-hard combinatorial or non-convex optimizations. Traditional solvers are often problem-specific and computationally heavy, while black-box learning methods fail to exploit the inherent relational structure between variables and constraints, leading to poor generalization. A framework that explicitly models this structure is needed to develop efficient, generalizable, and interpretable learning-based solvers.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly encoding the bipartite graph relationship between optimization variables and problem constraints, Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) can learn solution strategies that generalize across problem instances and scales more effectively than unstructured neural networks or traditional optimization methods alone.
proposals.method: We will formulate optimization problems (e.g., weighted sum-rate maximization) as bipartite graphs where one node type represents variables (e.g., transmit powers) and the other represents constraints (e.g., SINR, power limits), with edges denoting participation. A message-passing GNN architecture (e.g., GraphSAGE or GAT) will operate on this graph to iteratively refine variable embeddings. The final node representations will be decoded into a solution (e.g., power values) using a multi-layer perceptron, trained end-to-end via a loss function combining objective value and constraint violation penalties, using unsupervised learning or expert solver demonstrations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, unifying GNN-based framework for structured learning in network optimization, demonstrating improved sample efficiency and generalization to unseen network topologies and sizes compared to baseline methods. It will yield a principled methodology for injecting prior algorithmic and structural knowledge into machine learning models for physical-layer and resource allocation problems.
proposals.novelty: While GNNs have been used for node-level prediction in networks, their application to explicitly model the bipartite variable-constraint structure of optimization problems is novel. This moves beyond using GNNs solely for network topology to encode the mathematical structure of the problem itself, creating a direct bridge between optimization theory and geometric deep learning.

proposals.title: Meta-Optimization for Wireless Networks: A Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning Framework for Rapid Adaptation to Dynamic Resource Allocation Problems
proposals.motivation: Modern wireless networks, including those using intelligent metasurfaces, face dynamic environments where optimal resource allocation problems (e.g., power control, beamforming) change rapidly with user mobility and traffic patterns. Traditional optimization methods or single-task learning models require costly re-computation or retraining for each new problem instance, leading to latency and inefficiency. A core challenge is developing algorithms that can leverage past problem-solving experience to adapt to new, unseen network configurations with minimal data and time.
