proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) framework can learn a high-quality initialization for a neural network-based optimizer, enabling rapid fine-tuning to solve new, related network optimization problems with significantly fewer gradient steps and less task-specific data than training from scratch.
proposals.method: We will formulate a distribution of network optimization tasks (e.g., varying user locations, channel conditions, and QoS demands) based on stochastic geometric models. A neural network will be trained as a parameterized optimizer, mapping network state to resource allocation decisions. The core technical approach is to apply the MAML algorithm to this optimizer, where the outer-loop meta-training minimizes the expected loss across tasks, and the inner-loop adapts via a few gradient steps. We will specifically investigate the integration of problem structure (e.g., convexity constraints) into the network architecture and loss design to improve meta-generalization.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel meta-learning framework tailored for wireless network optimization, demonstrating quantifiable reductions in adaptation time and data requirement for new problem instances. It will provide theoretical insights into the convergence of MAML in constrained, non-convex optimization settings common in communications. The resulting adaptive solver will be a key step towards truly 'cognitive' networks that self-optimize in real-time.
proposals.novelty: While MAML has been applied in computer vision and robotics, its application to rapidly adapt to new instances of constrained engineering optimization problems in communications is novel. The key innovation is the fusion of meta-learning principles with domain-specific knowledge of network problem structure, moving beyond generic black-box adaptation to a problem-aware, sample-efficient meta-optimizer.

proposals.title: Problem-Aware Deep Learning for Scalable Optimization in Massive-Scale Wireless Networks
proposals.motivation: The optimization of ultra-dense networks (UDNs) and continent-scale topologies, such as those envisioned for 6G and massive IoT, presents prohibitive computational complexity for traditional solvers. Pre-2026, learning-based methods offer promise but often lack scalability and fail to exploit the inherent mathematical structure of communication and resource allocation problems. A principled approach is needed to bridge high-performance optimization with the scale of next-generation networks.
proposals.hypothesis: By embedding problem-specific constraints and optimality structures (e.g., Karush-Kuhn-Tucker conditions, interference coupling models) directly into a deep neural architecture, a Problem-Aware Deep (PAD) learning framework can achieve near-optimal performance for massive-scale network problems with orders-of-magnitude lower online computational cost than conventional optimization methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a graph neural network (GNN) architecture whose propagation rules are informed by the physical interference model and optimization Lagrangian of problems like massive MIMO beamforming or multi-cell resource allocation. The training loss will combine task-specific objectives with regularization terms derived from optimality conditions. We will train the model using a curriculum learning strategy on progressively larger synthetic network topologies, generated to mimic ultra-dense urban and continent-scale deployments, and evaluate on real-world topology data from pre-2026 wireless testbeds.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a scalable PAD optimization framework validated on continent-scale network problems, demonstrating a concrete path to real-time management of ultra-dense 6G systems. It will provide theoretical insights into the sample complexity and generalization bounds of problem-aware learning when applied to constrained, large-scale network optimization.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic integration of large-scale network topology characteristics and optimization-theoretic constraints into a single, scalable GNN-based PAD model, moving beyond proof-of-concept on small problems to address the fundamental scalability challenge for real-world deployment.

proposals.title: Problem-Aware Learning for Efficient Dual Lagrangian Optimization in Wireless Networks
proposals.motivation: Many core problems in wireless network optimization, such as resource allocation and beamforming, are formulated as constrained optimization tasks, often tackled via their Lagrangian duals. Solving these dual problems often requires iterative subgradient or primal-dual methods, which can be slow to converge and sensitive to parameter tuning. A more efficient, adaptive solver for dual formulations is needed to enable real-time network control in complex systems like intelligent metasurface-aided communications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a problem-aware learning framework, which embeds the known mathematical structure of the original primal problem into a neural network architecture, can learn to solve the corresponding dual Lagrangian problems with significantly faster convergence and greater robustness than generic iterative solvers.
proposals.method: We will develop a neural network architecture where the layers explicitly encode the constraints and objective of the primal problem. This network will be trained to map problem parameters (e.g., channel state information, user priorities) directly to optimal dual variables. The training loss will combine dual objective value maximization with a penalty for primal constraint violation, using the known primal solution structure to generate supervised data or guide reinforcement learning. Specific focus will be on dual problems arising in the downlink multiuser and direction-of-arrival estimation contexts from Dr. Poor's recent work.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, hybrid optimization-learning solver tailored for dual problems, demonstrating faster convergence in simulations. It will provide new insights into how problem structure can be baked into learning architectures for optimization, potentially extending the 'cross-problem solving' paradigm. The resulting models could be integrated into control loops for reconfigurable intelligent surfaces and other next-generation network elements.
proposals.novelty: While learning for primal optimization has been explored, the targeted application of problem-aware learning specifically to the dual domain is novel. The approach moves beyond using neural networks as black-box substitutes, instead designing them as structured dual solvers that leverage known Lagrangian mechanics.

proposals.title: Inverse Optimization via Latent Problem Inference: A Meta-Learning Framework for Network Resource Allocation
proposals.motivation: In complex network systems like wireless communications and smart grids, the underlying optimization problem (e.g., objective and constraints) is often not fully explicit to an observer or a learning agent. However, one can frequently observe sets of candidate or historical solutions, such as past resource allocations or beamforming configurations. The ability to infer the latent optimization problem from such data is crucial for understanding system behavior, diagnosing suboptimal performance, and enabling rapid adaptation to new, unseen problem instances.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the structure of a parametric optimization problem (e.g., a quadratic program or a semidefinite program) governing a network system can be accurately reconstructed from a set of observed solution samples by learning a mapping from solution sets to problem parameters within a meta-learning framework.
proposals.method: We propose a bi-level optimization approach. At the inner level, we model the latent problem as a parametric convex optimization problem (e.g., with an unknown positive semidefinite cost matrix and linear constraints). At the outer level, we train a neural network—specifically, a Deep Set architecture or a Transformer encoder—to ingest a set of observed solution vectors and output the estimated problem parameters. The training loss minimizes the discrepancy between the observed solutions and the optimal solutions of the inferred problem, solved via differentiable convex optimization layers (e.g., CVXPYlayers). We will validate the method on datasets generated from wireless network problems, including power control and beamforming.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, principled framework for inverse optimization in networked systems, moving beyond point estimation to set-based problem inference. It will provide a new tool for network diagnostics and a foundation for 'problem-aware' meta-learning, where an agent can generalize to new tasks by first inferring the governing problem. The resulting algorithms will be validated on canonical communication and grid optimization problems.
proposals.novelty: While inverse optimization is known, the focus on inferring a full parametric problem from a *set* of diverse candidate solutions, rather than a single optimal point, and the integration of this with meta-learning architectures for generalization across problem families, is novel. This directly extends the PI's recent work on cross-problem solving and learning for networks.

proposals.title: Cost-Optimal Data Delivery in Hybrid Networks: A Constrained Inverse Offloading Framework
proposals.motivation: In hybrid networks combining cellular and opportunistic device-to-device (D2D) links, prior research focuses on maximizing offloading to reduce cellular load. However, with the rise of tiered data plans and network slicing, the operational cost is directly tied to cellular data volume, not just load. A formulation that explicitly minimizes cellular usage under a quality-of-service constraint is needed for cost-efficient network management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formulating data delivery as a constrained inverse optimization problem—minimizing cellular data cost subject to a target delivery ratio—we can derive resource allocation policies that are fundamentally more cost-efficient than traditional offloading-maximization approaches.
proposals.method: We will model the network as a time-varying graph where edges represent costly cellular links and stochastic, cost-free D2D opportunities. The core is a stochastic optimization problem with a chance constraint for the delivery ratio. We will develop a two-stage method: first, a learning-aided predictor for D2D contact patterns using graph neural networks; second, a model predictive control (MPC) solver that uses the predictions to solve a receding-horizon convex approximation of the original problem, determining the minimal necessary cellular transmissions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new optimization framework for cost-aware hybrid networking, a set of practical algorithms for dynamic resource allocation, and quantitative benchmarks showing the cost savings versus pure offloading strategies. It directly contributes to the economics of 5G/6G network operation and dense IoT deployments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the classic offloading objective to a cost-minimization problem under a probabilistic delivery guarantee, explicitly integrating cellular data cost as the primary optimization variable rather than a secondary metric.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Federated QD-Learning for Scalable Network Optimization in Massive Multi-Agent Systems
proposals.motivation: Multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) and specifically QD-learning have shown promise for distributed network optimization, but their centralized or fully cooperative architectures suffer from exponential complexity and communication overhead as agent populations scale to thousands, as envisioned in ultra-dense IoT and 6G networks. Pre-2026 work lacks efficient, scalable frameworks that maintain performance guarantees for such massive decentralized systems, creating a critical bottleneck for real-world deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical, clustered federated learning architecture for QD-learning, where agents within clusters perform local consensus QD-updates and cluster heads engage in a higher-level federated averaging process, will achieve near-optimal network performance with sub-linear scaling of communication and computational complexity.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-tier hierarchical algorithm. First, we partition the massive network into clusters using spectral clustering or community detection based on the network's coupling matrix. Within each cluster, agents execute a consensus QD-learning algorithm (e.g., a diffusion or ADMM-based variant) to estimate a local Q-function. Second, cluster heads aggregate their local Q-function parameters (or sufficient statistics) via a federated averaging scheme, periodically synchronizing a global model. We will rigorously analyze the convergence using stochastic approximation theory and Lyapunov methods, characterizing the trade-off between cluster size, communication frequency, and optimality gap.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first principled framework for scaling QD-learning to thousands of agents with provable efficiency-performance trade-offs. It will deliver a concrete algorithmic toolkit for massive distributed network optimization problems, such as resource allocation in ultra-dense RANs or large-scale demand response in smart grids, directly impacting the design of scalable intelligent metasurface-aided networks.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of hierarchical clustering with federated learning principles into the QD-learning paradigm, moving beyond flat cooperation structures, to address the curse of dimensionality in massive agent systems is a non-obvious and significant advance over pre-2026 MARL methods.

proposals.title: Fundamental Limits and Optimal Strategies for Two-Agent Cooperative Learning in Networked Systems
proposals.motivation: Cooperative multi-agent learning is central to modern networked systems, from distributed sensing to wireless resource allocation. However, the complexity of general multi-agent algorithms often obscures fundamental performance limits and complicates analysis. Focusing on the minimal, irreducible two-agent case provides a tractable yet powerful model to derive foundational insights into cooperation, competition for resources, and information exchange, which can later scale to larger networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by specializing general multi-agent optimization frameworks to the two-agent case, we can derive closed-form or near-optimal solutions, establish sharp performance bounds, and reveal a fundamental trade-off between learning efficiency and communication cost that is obfuscated in larger-scale analyses.
proposals.method: We will formulate the two-agent cooperative learning problem as a constrained bi-level optimization, where agents jointly minimize a global loss function subject to individual resource constraints (e.g., power, bandwidth). Leveraging Professor Poor's expertise in matrix methods and optimization, we will employ techniques like alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) and consensus optimization, but with the problem structure simplified to two agents. This allows for analytical decomposition of the solution matrix and the potential derivation of optimal policies using dynamic programming or game-theoretic equilibrium concepts tailored for dyadic interaction.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a rigorously analyzed, benchmark algorithm for the two-agent cooperative learning problem, serving as a canonical reference. It will provide provable convergence guarantees and explicit performance bounds, offering a foundational building block for understanding and designing more complex, hierarchical, or federated learning systems where pairwise interaction is a core component.
proposals.novelty: While multi-agent learning is well-studied, a dedicated, in-depth treatment of the two-agent case as a fundamental unit of cooperation is lacking. The novelty lies in exploiting this minimal structure not just for simplification, but to uncover analytical results and fundamental limits that are intractable for N>2, providing new theoretical grounding for dyadic interactions in networks.

proposals.title: Beyond Distribution Learning: Constraint-Aware and Feasibility-Preserving Generative Models for Network Optimization with Imperfect Data
proposals.motivation: In wireless network optimization, learning-based methods often assume training data is drawn from the true, optimal policy distribution. Real-world data, however, is frequently suboptimal, incomplete, or non-stationary, causing distribution learning to produce infeasible or highly degraded solutions. This mismatch limits the deployment of data-driven methods in critical areas like resource allocation and beamforming, where constraint satisfaction is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that reformulating the generative task as a constrained feasibility search or a regret minimization over a historical solution set will yield more robust and implementable policies than direct distribution learning when trained on suboptimal data.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage neural architecture. First, a feasibility-projection network, inspired by primal-dual learning frameworks, will map any input (including suboptimal historical solutions) to the feasible set defined by hard network constraints. Second, a refinement transformer will perform iterative, context-aware improvements on this feasible point, guided by a learned regret estimator comparing against historical data. This approach will be evaluated on multiuser MIMO downlink problems and intelligent metasurface configuration tasks using synthetic and real-world suboptimal datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new methodological framework for solution generation under data imperfections, explicitly decoupling feasibility guarantees from distribution fitting. It will deliver practical algorithms for operators with legacy or noisy data, advancing the robustness of learning-for-optimization paradigms in communications.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly abandoning the goal of learning the data distribution, instead treating suboptimal data as a constraint set for a feasibility-guided generative process, which is a significant departure from prevailing generative adversarial or variational autoencoder-based approaches in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Decentralized Diffusion Models for Network Optimization: A Federated Learning Framework
proposals.motivation: Diffusion models have shown remarkable success in generating high-quality solutions for complex optimization problems in wireless networks, such as beamforming and resource allocation. However, their centralized training paradigm requires aggregating sensitive, high-dimensional channel state information from all users at a single server, raising significant privacy, communication overhead, and scalability concerns in large-scale distributed networks. A decentralized approach is critical for practical deployment in next-generation networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a federated learning framework, where clients collaboratively train a diffusion model by sharing only model updates rather than raw data, can generate high-fidelity, near-optimal solutions for network optimization problems while preserving data privacy and reducing communication costs.
proposals.method: We will develop a federated denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM) tailored for network optimization tasks. The central server will maintain a global diffusion model for solution generation. Each network node (client) will perform local training on its private channel data using a stochastic differential equation (SDE)-based solver, computing gradients for the denoising network. We will employ federated averaging (FedAvg) with adaptive client selection and gradient clipping to aggregate updates. The training objective will be regularized to align the generated solutions (e.g., beamforming matrices) with network-wide optimality conditions derived from constrained optimization theory.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first federated learning framework for training diffusion models specifically for communication and network optimization problems. It will deliver a practical algorithm that balances solution optimality with privacy and communication efficiency, directly applicable to distributed systems like cell-free networks and intelligent metasurface-aided communications. The theoretical analysis will establish convergence guarantees and privacy bounds for the proposed federated diffusion process.
proposals.novelty: While federated learning for discriminative models is well-studied, its application to generative diffusion models for solving parametric physical-layer optimization problems is novel. The key innovation lies in adapting the diffusion process to generate structured network parameters (e.g., covariance matrices) and ensuring the federated training converges to a model that respects distributed network constraints.

proposals.title: Generalized Deep Stochastic Geometry for NP-Hard Optimization in Multi-Access Edge Computing Networks
proposals.motivation: Multi-access edge computing (MEC) networks face several NP-hard optimization problems, such as joint service placement and request routing, resource-aware task scheduling, and dynamic network slicing. Solving these problems with traditional optimization is intractable for large-scale, dynamic networks. While the GDSG (Generalized Deep Stochastic Geometry) framework has shown promise for computation offloading, its potential for a broader class of MEC challenges remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the GDSG framework, which integrates stochastic geometry for network modeling with deep learning for policy optimization, can be generalized to efficiently and near-optimally solve other core NP-hard optimization problems in MEC networks.
proposals.method: We will first formalize the problem structures for target challenges (e.g., service placement, network slicing) into a Markov Decision Process compatible with GDSG. The core technical extension involves designing novel neural network architectures that map stochastic geometry-based network state representations (e.g., point processes of access points, users, and servers) to joint action spaces for the specific problem. We will employ advanced policy gradient methods, proximal policy optimization (PPO), and integrate graph neural networks to capture topological dependencies for problems like routing and placement.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a unified, scalable algorithmic framework for a suite of MEC optimization problems, validated through simulation against state-of-the-art heuristics and lower bounds. It will provide new theoretical insights into the representational power of stochastic geometry for learning-based network control.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in systematically abstracting and extending the GDSG paradigm beyond offloading to diverse problem classes, creating a new 'cross-problem' learning methodology for MEC, rather than developing a single bespoke solution.

proposals.title: Generalizable Graph Meta-Learning for Robust Optimization in Multi-Access Edge Computing Networks
proposals.motivation: The dynamic and heterogeneous nature of Multi-Access Edge Computing (MEC) networks, with fluctuating user demands and network states, poses a significant challenge for static optimization models. While Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have shown promise for network-aware optimization, they often struggle to generalize across unseen network topologies or task distributions, limiting their practical deployment. Enhancing GNN generalization is thus critical for developing robust, real-time resource allocation and task offloading solutions in MEC.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating meta-learning principles with a problem-aware, multi-relational GNN architecture will enable learning of transferable optimization policies that generalize effectively across diverse MEC network configurations and problem instances.
proposals.method: We will develop a Meta-Graph Neural Network (Meta-GNN) framework. The core architecture will be a relational GNN (R-GNN) that explicitly models different edge types (e.g., communication links, computational dependencies, interference). This encoder will be coupled with a meta-learning outer loop based on Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML), which will train the model on a distribution of simulated MEC problem instances (varying topologies, user distributions, QoS requirements). The inner-loop adaptation will allow rapid fine-tuning to new, specific network scenarios using limited local data.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, generalizable learning framework for MEC network optimization that moves beyond single-problem training. It will provide concrete algorithmic advancements in combining relational inductive biases with meta-learning for networking. The resulting models are expected to demonstrate superior performance and sample efficiency when adapting to new network conditions compared to conventional GNNs and optimization solvers.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic integration of problem-aware relational GNNs with gradient-based meta-learning specifically tailored for the structured optimization problems inherent to MEC networks, addressing the generalization gap in a principled manner not explored in prior GNN-for-networks literature.

proposals.title: Efficient Suboptimal Data Generation via Meta-Learned Heuristics for Network Optimization
proposals.motivation: Learning-aided network optimization often requires large datasets of suboptimal solutions for training, but generating these datasets with traditional heuristics is computationally expensive. This bottleneck limits the scalability and practicality of problem-aware learning frameworks, especially for real-time applications in wireless communications and resource allocation. An efficient method for generating diverse, high-quality suboptimal data is therefore critical for advancing data-driven optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-learning framework can discover efficient, adaptive heuristics for generating suboptimal training data, significantly reducing computational cost while preserving or improving the diversity and quality of the generated dataset compared to fixed, handcrafted heuristics.
proposals.method: We will formulate the heuristic design as a meta-optimization problem. A recurrent neural network (RNN) or graph neural network (GNN) meta-controller will be trained via reinforcement learning to adaptively modify parameters or select steps of a base heuristic (e.g., a greedy algorithm or local search) for specific network problem instances. The training signal will balance solution cost, diversity, and generation speed. This will be tested on canonical problems like power allocation in downlink systems and sensor selection, using the generated data to train downstream task-specific models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, automated framework for creating training data for learning-based optimizers, reducing a key barrier to their adoption. It will yield a reusable meta-heuristic generator that can be adapted to various network problems, improving the Pareto frontier of data quality versus generation cost. The results will directly enhance the training phase of the problem-aware learning paradigm.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been used for optimizing algorithms, its application to specifically improve the *data generation process* for training other learning-based optimizers is novel. This shifts the focus from learning the solver directly to learning how to efficiently create the curriculum for the solver.

proposals.title: Generating Diverse Training Data for Network Optimization via Reinforcement Learning-Guided Solution Space Exploration
proposals.motivation: Data-driven methods for network optimization, such as power allocation or user scheduling, often require large, high-quality datasets of problem instances and solutions. However, generating such data via conventional solvers is computationally expensive, and the resulting data may lack diversity, covering only near-optimal points. This limits the generalization and robustness of learned models, a critical bottleneck in deploying learning for real-time network control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a reinforcement learning (RL) agent, trained with a reward function that balances solution quality against novelty, can efficiently explore the solution space of complex network optimization problems to generate a diverse and informative dataset of suboptimal solutions, which will subsequently improve the performance and sample efficiency of supervised learning models.
proposals.method: We will formulate the solution space exploration as a Markov Decision Process where the state is the current candidate solution and the action perturbs it. An RL agent, using a policy gradient method like Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), will be trained with a composite reward: a primary term from the network problem's objective (e.g., sum-rate) and a secondary novelty bonus computed via a running archive of discovered solutions. The agent's trajectories will be logged to create a dataset of (problem parameters, diverse solution) pairs. This dataset will then be used to train a fast neural network approximator.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, automated framework for generating high-quality training data for learning-based network optimization, reducing reliance on exhaustive numerical solvers. It will demonstrate improved sample efficiency and final performance of learned policies in downstream tasks like power control and beamforming, validated on standard multi-user MIMO and intelligent metasurface system models.
proposals.novelty: While RL is often used to learn policies directly, its novel application here is as an intelligent data-generation engine for supervised learning. The key innovation is the explicit shaping of the RL reward to maximize solution diversity, not just optimality, creating a richer training corpus than standard methods.

proposals.title: Bayesian Optimization for Hyperparameter Tuning in Graph Diffusion Models: A Cross-Problem Learning Framework for Network Optimization
proposals.motivation: The integration of graph neural networks (GNNs) with diffusion models has shown promise for complex network optimization tasks, such as resource allocation and beamforming design. However, the joint hyperparameter space of these models is high-dimensional and expensive to evaluate, making manual or grid search intractable. Efficiently navigating this space is critical for deploying these learning-based solutions in practical, real-time wireless networks and sensing systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a problem-aware Bayesian optimization (BO) framework, which incorporates domain knowledge into its surrogate model and acquisition function, can efficiently identify near-optimal hyperparameters for graph diffusion models, leading to superior performance on downstream network optimization tasks compared to standard tuning methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a BO framework where the objective function is the validation performance of a GNN-coupled diffusion model on a target network problem (e.g., power control). The surrogate model will be a Gaussian process, but its kernel will be designed to encode structural similarities between different network problem instances, drawing on the 'cross-problem solving' concept. We will employ an expected improvement acquisition function, modified with a cost-aware term for computational efficiency. The method will be tested on benchmarks including wireless interference management and DoA estimation tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, automated tuning strategy for state-of-the-art graph-based generative models in communications. It will yield new insights into the hyperparameter sensitivity of these models for physical-layer problems. The resulting framework will be a step towards practical, adaptive optimization tools for next-generation intelligent metasurface-aided networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in tailoring Bayesian optimization for the joint hyperparameter tuning of diffusion and GNN models specifically for network optimization, moving beyond generic ML tasks. Integrating cross-problem structural knowledge into the BO kernel to accelerate tuning across related wireless problems is a non-obvious extension of prior work.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning for Fast Adaptation of Optimization Policies in Dynamic Mobile Edge Computing Networks
proposals.motivation: Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) networks are highly dynamic, with frequent changes in user locations, channel conditions, and compute task arrivals. Pre-2026 optimization methods, while powerful, often require time-consuming re-calibration or re-training for each new network configuration, hindering real-time performance. A framework for rapid adaptation is crucial for efficient resource allocation, task offloading, and interference management in next-generation networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-learning framework, trained on a distribution of MEC network optimization problems, can learn a model initialization that enables fast adaptation—with only a few gradient steps—to high-performance policies for previously unseen network configurations.
proposals.method: We will formulate MEC resource allocation (e.g., joint communication and computation optimization) as a parameterized reinforcement learning problem. We will employ a Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) framework, where the inner-loop tasks correspond to distinct network configurations (varying user density, path-loss models, server loads). The meta-learner will be implemented using a graph neural network (GNN) to process the network topology, with the goal of outputting an initialization for a policy network. This initialization will be fine-tuned online via a few steps of policy gradient for a new, specific network instance.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a practical meta-learning algorithm tailored for network optimization, demonstrating significantly reduced adaptation time compared to training from scratch or using transfer learning. It will provide a principled framework for 'cross-problem solving' in MEC, moving towards generalizable and agile network intelligence.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been explored in generic RL settings, its application to MEC network optimization is nascent. The novelty lies in the integration of GNN-based problem encoding with MAML to explicitly capture the topological and parametric structure of MEC problems, enabling adaptation not just to new data but to new network graph instances and constraint sets.

proposals.title: Problem-Aware Learning for Joint Computation and Communication Optimization in Single-Server Edge Networks
proposals.motivation: Edge computing optimization for multi-user, multi-server networks is well-studied, but the single-server scenario, common in small-cell deployments and private industrial networks, is often oversimplified or treated as a trivial special case. Pre-2026, the complexity of joint radio resource allocation and computational offloading scheduling for a single resource-constrained server serving multiple heterogeneous users remains a significant barrier to efficient, real-time implementation. A dedicated, simplified model is needed to unlock ultra-low-latency solutions for this fundamental topology.
proposals.hypothesis: By developing a simplified, single-server optimization model and applying a problem-aware learning framework, we can derive lightweight, near-optimal algorithms that outperform both generic multi-server simplifications and conventional convex optimization solvers in terms of computational speed and solution quality for latency-critical tasks.
proposals.method: We will first formulate a simplified mixed-integer optimization problem focusing on joint uplink/downlink communication scheduling and CPU cycle allocation for a single edge server. We will then employ a problem-aware learning architecture, potentially using a graph neural network (GNN) to encode user-server dependencies and a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) agent trained on the specific problem structure. The learning model will be designed to exploit the reduced dimensionality of the single-server constraint matrix, enabling fast inference. Performance will be benchmarked against traditional methods like successive convex approximation (SCA) and generic DRL baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a foundational optimization framework tailored for single-server edge networks, filling a gap in the literature. It will deliver a computationally efficient algorithm that balances optimality and latency, directly applicable to practical deployments. The research will also demonstrate the efficacy of problem-aware learning in exploiting structural simplifications for enhanced performance.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate downscaling and specialization of the network model to the single-server case, which is non-trivial, and the co-design of a lightweight learning architecture that leverages this specific problem structure, moving beyond simply applying generic multi-user algorithms.

proposals.title: Inverse Network Design: Inferring MEC Resource Capacity Requirements from Optimal Offloading Strategies
proposals.motivation: Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) network planning traditionally involves provisioning resources (compute, bandwidth) and then optimizing task offloading. This forward approach is inefficient and may lead to over-provisioning or performance bottlenecks. The inverse problem—deducing the minimal necessary resource capacities from a set of desired optimal offloading solutions—is crucial for cost-effective, demand-driven network design, especially with the rise of latency-critical applications and dense IoT deployments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the mapping from a set of optimal offloading solutions (e.g., for varying user/task distributions) to the underlying required network resource capacities is learnable using a constrained optimization framework, and that this inferred capacity profile is both sufficient and near-minimal for supporting the target performance.
proposals.method: We will formulate the inverse problem as a constrained matrix recovery and regression task. Given a dataset of optimal offloading decision matrices (from solving forward problems), we will train a Graph Neural Network (GNN) enhanced with attention mechanisms to capture network topology and task dependencies. The GNN will output predicted capacity vectors (compute at nodes, bandwidth on links). This will be integrated with an adversarial or bi-level optimization layer to ensure the predicted capacities are feasible and induce the original optimal solutions when used as constraints in a standard offloading solver.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel data-driven methodology for proactive and efficient MEC network dimensioning. It will yield a practical tool for network operators to translate service-level objectives into infrastructure requirements. Theoretically, it will advance the understanding of inverse problems in networked systems and the integration of learning with constrained optimization.
proposals.novelty: While forward offloading optimization is well-studied, the explicit learning-based inversion to infer physical resource constraints from optimal solutions is largely unexplored. The novelty lies in combining problem-aware learning (inspired by the researcher's prior work) with adversarial feasibility guarantees for a network design task.

proposals.title: Joint Optimization of Computation Offloading and Wireless Resource Allocation via Problem-Aware Meta-Learning
proposals.motivation: The performance of mobile edge computing (MEC) systems is fundamentally limited by the tight coupling between computational task requirements and the dynamics of the wireless channel. Prior work often treats offloading decisions and resource allocation (e.g., power, bandwidth, beamforming) as sequential or loosely coupled sub-problems, leading to sub-optimal system efficiency, especially in dense, heterogeneous networks. A truly joint optimization framework is critical for next-generation networks with stringent latency and energy constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a problem-aware meta-learning framework can efficiently solve the joint, non-convex optimization of computation offloading and multi-dimensional wireless resource allocation, learning to map network state (channel conditions, queue states) to near-optimal joint policies that outperform decoupled or iterative approaches.
proposals.method: We will formulate the joint problem as a two-timescale stochastic optimization, where fast-timescale resource allocation (modeled via a weighted sum-rate maximization with interference) is embedded within slow-timescale offloading decisions. We propose a novel solution architecture combining a meta-learning outer loop, trained on a distribution of network problem instances (varying user densities, task graphs), with an inner loop based on deep unfolding of an iterative optimization solver (e.g., a matrix-based WMMSE variant for resource allocation). This 'learning-to-optimize' approach will use graph neural networks to encode the network topology and task dependencies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable algorithmic framework for joint MEC system optimization, demonstrating significant gains in total system cost (latency + energy) over state-of-the-art baselines. It will contribute a new problem-aware meta-learning methodology for cross-problem solving in network optimization, specifically bridging communication and computation resource management.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight integration of a meta-learned policy for high-level offloading with a deep-unfolded, interpretable wireless optimization module, moving beyond black-box end-to-end learning. This explicitly leverages the mathematical structure of both the communication and computation sub-problems within a single trainable architecture.

ideas.title: Problem formulated as distribution learning
ideas.description: Explore alternative problem formulations beyond distribution learning for solution generation with suboptimal data.
ideas.sourceTitle: GDSG: Graph Diffusion-Based Solution Generator for Optimization Problems in MEC Networks

ideas.title: Apply Multi-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning with Centralized Training for D2D Power and Rate Control
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from Intelligent Transportation Systems, specifically Multi-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning (MADRL) with centralized critics used for cooperative traffic signal control. The analogous problem involved multiple autonomous agents (traffic lights) needing to make local decisions to optimize a global objective (network-wide traffic flow), which parallels multiple D2D links needing to autonomously control power and data rates to optimize network capacity while collectively managing interference to primary users. The adapted idea applies the Centralized Training with Decentralized Execution (CTDE) framework, using an algorithm like Multi-Agent Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (MADDPG). Each D2D link acts as an independent agent that executes a local policy based on its own channel state and limited observations. However, during the training phase, a centralized critic network, privy to the global state of the cellular underlay (including aggregate interference and primary user feedback), is used to guide the learning of all agents' policies. This enables the decentralized D2D links to learn cooperative behaviors—implicitly coordinating their power and rate selections—to maximize a network-wide objective such as sum spectral efficiency or total D2D throughput, while strictly adhering to the dynamic interference constraints imposed by the primary cellular network.

ideas.title: Evolutionary-Guided Stochastic Optimization for Large-Scale Structured Matrix Problems
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a novel hybrid optimizer for large-scale or online optimization problems with matrix-monotonic structure. The core idea is to use a metaheuristic population (e.g., PSO) not to search the primary parameter space directly, but to adaptively optimize the configuration of a concurrent stochastic gradient method. The evolutionary layer would dynamically tune hyperparameters like learning rates, momentum, and, crucially, the importance sampling distribution for mini-batch selection to respect the matrix structure, which the SGD layer alone cannot efficiently discover. This enables robust, structure-aware optimization in non-stationary or very high-dimensional environments where pure SGD often struggles with hyperparameter sensitivity and pure evolutionary algorithms are too slow.

ideas.title: Robust Deep Reinforcement Learning for D2D Underlay Networks with Imperfect Channel Distribution Information
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel robust deep reinforcement learning (DRL) framework for joint power and rate control in dynamic underlay D2D networks. The core innovation is to integrate robust optimization principles directly into the DRL agent's objective and state representation to handle bounded imperfections in the Channel Distribution Information (CDI). The agent learns a policy that optimizes long-term network performance while explicitly accounting for the worst-case statistical deviations within a predefined uncertainty set, ensuring robustness. This enables real-time, adaptive decision-making that is resilient to model errors and distributional shifts, a capability unattainable by applying either DRL or robust optimization in isolation.

ideas.title: Minimax Power Control: Minimizing Peak Power or Harvested Energy for Guaranteed Performance
ideas.description: This research formulates and solves the dual problem of minimizing the required peak power or the required energy harvesting capability (e.g., battery size, harvester rating) subject to a hard constraint on system performance, such as a minimum data rate or maximum distortion. This shifts the design paradigm from optimizing average performance under an average power budget to provisioning the minimal instantaneous resource infrastructure needed to reliably meet a quality-of-service target. The core challenge involves solving stochastic optimization problems where the constraint is on a temporal extremum (peak) or an integral of a stochastic process (harvested energy), rather than an expectation. This leads to novel control policies that explicitly manage performance risk against resource volatility, with applications in energy-harvesting IoT networks, burst-communication systems, and power-grid-constrained edge computing.

ideas.title: Adaptive Compressed Sensing for CSI Feedback via Neuromorphic Predictive Coding
ideas.description: This idea solves the inverse problem of efficient CSI feedback by drawing an analogy from how the brain's sensory systems use predictive coding for efficient data transmission. In computational neuroscience, predictive coding posits that the brain creates an internal model to predict sensory input and only communicates the prediction errors, which are typically sparse and require less bandwidth. Adapting this solution, we propose that the User Equipment (UE) learns a generative model of the wireless channel's temporal and spatial structure. The UE continuously predicts the channel state and computes a compressed representation of the prediction error. This sparse error signal, rather than the full channel matrix, is fed back to the gNodeB (gNB). The gNB, possessing a synchronized version of the generative model, uses the error signal to accurately reconstruct the full CSI. This approach leverages the optimal structure (the learned generative model) to design an adaptive, highly efficient feedback strategy that scales with channel correlation and dynamics, minimizing overhead while preserving critical information.

ideas.title: Stochastic Optimization for Matrix-Monotonic Problems with General Linear Constraints
ideas.description: This research direction investigates stochastic first-order methods tailored for matrix-monotonic optimization problems subject to broad classes of linear constraints, such as affine equality/inequality constraints on the matrix elements or its eigenvalues, and linear operator constraints arising in system design. By leveraging the inherent structure of matrix-monotonic functions, the work will develop stochastic gradient or proximal algorithms that efficiently handle very large datasets or streaming data where the constraints themselves may be estimated from samples. The core synergy lies in extending the scalability of stochastic methods beyond simple domains to complex constrained matrix manifolds, while simultaneously generalizing the theory of matrix-monotonic optimization to stochastic and online settings. This enables novel applications in adaptive filter design, large-scale covariance matrix estimation under structural constraints, and robust online resource allocation for networked systems.

ideas.title: Riemannian Manifold Optimization for Predictive Resource Allocation in Dynamic Mobility Networks
ideas.description: This research fuses dynamic user mobility modeling with Riemannian optimization to create a predictive framework for wireless resource allocation. It models the sequence of expected channel states, influenced by user trajectories, as a smooth curve on a specific matrix manifold (e.g., the manifold of positive semidefinite matrices with rank constraints). By solving optimization problems directly on this manifold, the approach inherently respects the structural constraints of the communication system while leveraging the geometric properties of mobility to predict future interference and channel conditions. This enables the proactive computation of optimal beamforming, power, and spectrum allocation policies that are both highly efficient and robust to the anticipated dynamics of the network.

ideas.title: DRL-Optimized CSI Feedback for Dynamic Underlay D2D Resource Coexistence
ideas.description: This research fuses CSI feedback design with deep reinforcement learning to create an adaptive, dual-loop control system for underlay D2D networks. A DRL agent is tasked with jointly optimizing the transmission policies (power, rate) for D2D pairs and the structure, frequency, and precision of the CSI feedback they provide to the cellular base station. By learning the value of different CSI feedback characteristics (e.g., quantization levels, reporting periodicity) for its own decision-making under dynamic interference, the agent can request feedback that is optimally compressed and context-aware. This enables a synergistic co-design where feedback efficiency and resource allocation policy are learned simultaneously, maximizing sum throughput while minimizing signaling overhead in highly dynamic environments.

ideas.title: Matrix-Monotonic Stochastic Mirror Descent for Online Portfolio Optimization
ideas.description: This research adapts solution concepts from theoretical ecology—specifically, the replicator dynamics and its stochastic, discrete-time implementations—to the problem of large-scale online optimization with matrix-monotonic structure. The analogous ecological problem involves evolving a population distribution on a simplex under stochastic fitness signals, solved using multiplicative update rules that preserve feasibility and exhibit natural monotonic convergence. Adapted to the optimization context, we propose a novel stochastic mirror descent framework for online problems where the variable is a positive definite matrix (e.g., in covariance estimation, metric learning, or quantum state tomography). The algorithm uses a Bregman divergence defined by a matrix-monotonic function, such as the log-determinant, leading to a multiplicative, exponentiated gradient update that automatically maintains the positive definite cone constraint without projection. This method inherently respects the Riemannian geometry of the positive definite manifold, aims to exploit geodesic convexity, and is designed for efficient, scalable implementation with stochastic gradient oracles.

ideas.title: Robust, Uncertainty-Aware Resource Allocation under Imperfect Mobility Prediction
ideas.description: This research designs a resource allocation framework that is robust to inherent inaccuracies in user mobility prediction. Instead of relying on a single assumed mobility model, the system learns to make allocation decisions by explicitly modeling and adapting to the uncertainty in future user locations and channel states. It employs techniques from distributionally robust optimization or meta-reinforcement learning to generalize across different and evolving mobility patterns without catastrophic performance loss. The outcome is a resilient network controller that maintains high efficiency and quality of service even when user movement deviates significantly from prior models or predictions.

ideas.title: Adapting Hydrological Reservoir Management to Communication Power Constraints
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the standard average power constraint in communication theory. Drawing an analogy from hydrology and water reservoir management, where system optimization revolves around reservoir capacity and stochastic inflows rather than average rainfall, we adapt the solution to communications. The adapted idea studies energy-harvesting communication nodes under peak power (battery capacity) and energy arrival constraints. By viewing the battery as a reservoir, we develop transmission policies analogous to hydrological rule curves. These policies dynamically adapt coding, modulation, and scheduling based on the current energy state and forecasts of energy harvesting, fundamentally shifting the optimization framework from average power to managing an energy inventory with stochastic replenishment, with the goal of maximizing data throughput or minimizing delay under this new constraint set.

ideas.title: Continuous Joint Optimization of RIS Phase-Shifts and Transceiver Power-Rate Adaptation
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel framework for optimizing reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS)-assisted wireless networks by jointly and continuously adapting the RIS phase-shift matrix alongside the transmitter's power and rate. It departs from traditional models that rely on discrete, limited-feedback quantization for both RIS configuration and link adaptation. By treating all control variables as continuous, the framework can formulate and solve a unified optimization problem for spectral or energy efficiency, capturing the full potential of the RIS-aided channel. This enables real-time, fine-grained adaptation to channel conditions, promising significant gains over systems where the RIS and transceiver are optimized separately or under discrete constraints.

ideas.title: Comparative Metaheuristic Analysis via Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'ecological niche' modeling framework from community ecology to conduct a nuanced comparison of Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) with other metaheuristics like Genetic Algorithms (GA) and Simulated Annealing (SA). In ecology, niche analysis quantifies how species partition resource gradients to coexist. Analogously, we propose mapping the 'performance niche' of each algorithm across a multivariate landscape of optimization problem features (e.g., modality, separability, dimensionality). By applying multivariate statistical and machine learning techniques to performance data from a diverse benchmark suite, we can systematically identify the specific problem characteristics (the 'niche axes') where each algorithm excels, where their performance niches overlap, and where they are complementary. This moves beyond simple head-to-head comparisons to a principled, explanatory framework for algorithm selection and hybrid design based on problem ecology.

ideas.title: Data-Adaptive Riemannian Optimization via Joint Geometric Inference and Robust Manifold Search
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the critical assumption of a known, perfect manifold by jointly learning the constraint geometry from data and performing optimization on it. The system uses techniques from manifold learning and geometric statistics to construct a probabilistic or regularized representation of the feasible set, which may be a smooth manifold, a stratified space, or a neighborhood of one. Optimization is then performed using robust Riemannian methods that account for the uncertainty in the learned geometry, ensuring convergence even when the true constraints are only approximately known or are subject to noise.

ideas.title: Adapt Sequential Convex Programming from Structural Design for Nonlinear Power Constraints
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept of incorporating nonlinear power constraints by drawing an analogy from structural topology optimization. In that field, Sequential Convex Programming (SCP) is a proven method for handling complex, nonlinear physical constraints like stress and displacement. We adapt this solution to wireless communications optimization. The core approach is to iteratively solve a sequence of convex approximations (e.g., trust-region based linearizations or convex restrictions) of the original non-convex problem defined by per-antenna power limits or amplitude saturation constraints. This provides a computationally tractable and convergent algorithm to design precoders and beamformers under realistic, nonlinear hardware limitations, directly challenging the common assumption of simple sum-power or convex quadratic constraints.

ideas.title: Shielded Deep Reinforcement Learning with a High-Fidelity Digital Twin for Safe Power and Rate Control in Underlay D2D Networks
ideas.description: This research proposes a safe and practical DRL framework for underlay D2D networks by first training agents in a high-fidelity, physics-based digital twin that accurately models radio propagation and primary network activity. A runtime safety shield, derived from robust interference constraints, is then deployed to monitor and correct all DRL actions in the real network, guaranteeing that interference thresholds are never breached. This allows the DRL agent to learn efficient policies through exploration in simulation and safe, limited adaptation online, without risking harmful disruption. The system thus achieves the performance benefits of DRL while providing formal safety assurances required for deployment in licensed spectrum.

ideas.description: This research develops a generalized imperfect CSI model family but crucially embeds it within a meta-learning system for model validation and selection. The system uses offline and online data to learn a mapping from observable channel environment features (e.g., coherence time, pilot contamination level) to the optimal model abstraction from the unified family for a given performance objective. This ensures the theoretical unification is grounded, provides concrete performance bounds for each model choice, and offers a practical tool for robust system design under uncertainty.

ideas.title: Robust RIS Phase-Shift Optimization Under Channel Uncertainty via Meta-Reinforcement Learning
ideas.description: This research redesigns the RIS optimization framework to explicitly address the critical challenge of imperfect and dynamic channel state information. It develops a two-timescale meta-reinforcement learning system where a meta-policy, trained over a distribution of channel statistics and error models, rapidly adapts a base RL agent to new environmental conditions. The agent optimizes the phase-shift matrix by directly observing network-level performance feedback, minimizing reliance on perfect instantaneous CSI. This results in a robust, self-correcting RIS controller that maintains high performance in realistic, uncertain wireless environments.

ideas.title: Multi-Objective Evolutionary Optimization of Full-Duplex D2D Jamming for Dynamic Secrecy
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel framework that uses a suite of evolutionary algorithms (EAs)—including Genetic Algorithms, Particle Swarm Optimization, and Differential Evolution—to dynamically optimize the transmit and jamming parameters for full-duplex D2D communication systems. The core objective is to maximize the secrecy rate while managing key constraints like self-interference cancellation, power budgets, and evolving eavesdropper locations. By benchmarking and hybridizing these metaheuristics, the study seeks to identify the most efficient adaptive optimizer for real-time adjustment of the jamming strategy in response to network dynamics, providing a robust optimization engine that is central to realizing the practical secrecy gains of full-duplex jamming.

ideas.title: Distributionally Robust Optimization for CDI Imperfections with Ambiguity Sets
ideas.description: This research proposes a distributionally robust optimization (DRO) framework to manage imperfect channel distribution information (CDI) in wireless systems. Instead of using a bounded uncertainty set for channel realizations, the method defines an ambiguity set of possible probability distributions for the channel, typically based on a distance metric like the Wasserstein distance from a nominal estimated distribution. The system then optimizes transmission parameters to maximize the worst-case expected performance (e.g., expected capacity or outage probability) over all distributions within this ambiguity set. This approach provides a rigorous and tunable balance between average performance and robustness, mitigating the excessive conservatism of standard robust optimization while being more practical than assuming perfect CDI.

ideas.title: Dual problem: Minimize power for performance
ideas.description: This research expands the original seed idea by drawing an analogy from thermodynamics and chemical engineering, specifically the solved problem of minimizing energy dissipation (or entropy production) for a required rate of a process, such as a chemical reaction or mechanical work output. The analogous solution uses constrained optimization (e.g., Lagrangian methods) from finite-time thermodynamics to find optimal control trajectories. Adapted to computer system scaling, the research idea is to formulate and solve the dual optimization problem of minimizing total power consumption given a hard performance target (e.g., a required number of instructions completed within a deadline). The solution will involve deriving the optimal dynamic control policy for system resources (like voltage/frequency), analogous to an optimal temperature path, that meets the performance constraint while minimizing the integral of power over time, moving beyond static operating points to time-adaptive scheduling.

ideas.title: Systematic Exploration of Intrinsic Order-Theoretic Linear Constraints in Matrix-Monotonic Optimization
ideas.description: This research systematically investigates linear constraints that are native to the order-theoretic framework of matrix-monotonic optimization, moving beyond generic linear constraints. It focuses on constraints of the form ∑_i A_i X_i ≼ ∑_j B_j Y_j, where ≼ is the Löwner order, ensuring the constraints directly interact with the monotonic objective function's domain. The approach classifies these intrinsic constraints by their algebraic structure and studies their impact on the convexity of the feasible set, the existence of spectral representations for solutions, and the tractability of resulting optimization problems. This yields a coherent theory of constrained matrix-monotonic optimization with direct applicability to problems in quantum resource theories and robust control.

ideas.title: Riemannian Optimization for Quantum State Tomography on Stiefel Manifolds
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept of using Riemannian optimization on matrix manifolds by drawing an analogy from Quantum Information Science. The analogous problem is Quantum State Tomography (QST), where the goal is to reconstruct a high-dimensional, physically constrained (positive semidefinite, trace-1) density matrix from measurements. This is solved by reformulating the optimization to occur directly on the manifold of valid quantum states (e.g., the fixed-rank positive semidefinite manifold) using Riemannian optimization techniques, ensuring constraints are inherently satisfied. We adapt this solution to the context of structured matrix optimization (e.g., low-rank matrix completion with additional structural constraints like being a product of structured factors). The adapted research idea is to formulate these structured matrix problems as optimization on specific product manifolds, such as the Stiefel manifold (for orthonormal components) cross a space of sparse or patterned matrices. By developing and applying Riemannian optimization algorithms (like Riemannian gradient or trust-region methods) on these custom manifolds, we can solve constrained matrix recovery problems more efficiently and reliably, ensuring the desired matrix structure is maintained throughout the optimization process, analogous to how QST maintains physical validity.

ideas.title: Joint Optimization of Training, Feedback, and Detection Under a Unified Receiver CSI Imperfection Budget
ideas.description: This research develops a novel communication framework that abandons the separate treatment of channel estimation and data detection. It introduces a 'CSI imperfection budget' that captures the combined cost of pilot energy, feedback bits, and computational complexity at the receiver. The core is a co-design optimization problem that allocates this budget between training and feedback phases to minimize the end-to-end system error rate or outage probability, directly deriving optimal receiver structures for data detection under the resulting imperfect CSI. This provides fundamental limits and practical algorithms for systems where perfect receiver CSI is not just unavailable but economically or physically suboptimal.

ideas.title: Extending MIMO Transceiver Design via Multi-Objective Optimization Inspired by Portfolio Theory
ideas.description: This idea proposes extending the MIMO transceiver design framework to a broader set of performance metrics by drawing an analogy from financial portfolio optimization. In Modern Portfolio Theory, the analogous problem of balancing multiple, often competing, objectives (return, risk, and others) is solved by constructing an efficient frontier via multi-objective optimization. Adapted to communications, this involves formulating a vector optimization problem where the design parameters (precoders, equalizers) are chosen to trace out the Pareto-optimal frontier in a space defined by metrics such as spectral efficiency, BER, energy consumption, latency, and robustness. This provides a systematic method to explore trade-offs and select optimal designs for diverse 5G/6G use cases, moving beyond optimization for a single or pre-weighted pair of metrics.

ideas.title: Dual-Optimal CSI Feedback: Minimizing Feedback Power for Target Estimation Performance
ideas.description: This research formulates and solves the dual problem of minimizing the transmit power (or energy) required for Channel State Information (CSI) feedback from a user equipment to a base station, given a target estimation performance metric (e.g., mean squared error or achievable rate loss). By analyzing the optimal structure of the solution to this dual power-minimization problem, we derive novel feedback strategies—encompassing quantization, compression, and scheduling—that are inherently energy-efficient. This enables the co-design of the feedback payload and the uplink transmission parameters, creating adaptive systems where feedback resource usage is dynamically minimized while guaranteeing the overall downlink performance.

ideas.title: Joint Energy Budget and Peak Power Constraint Framework for Communication and Sensing
ideas.description: This research develops a fundamental framework for resource allocation under a composite constraint consisting of a total energy budget over a timeframe and a strict peak power limit. The work characterizes the optimal signaling and policy design for problems in communications, estimation, and control under this generalized constraint. It demonstrates how the interplay between the energy and peak power boundaries defines the achievable region, recovering classical average-power and energy-harvesting results at limiting extremes. This provides a robust and practically complete model for systems with both finite energy stores and power amplifier or safety limitations.

ideas.title: Robust Optimization for a Unified Imperfect CSI Model Encompassing Distributional and Parametric Errors
ideas.description: This research synthesizes robust optimization techniques with a generalized imperfect Channel State Information (CSI) model. The unified model explicitly parameterizes both the instantaneous channel estimate's error (e.g., bounded norm error) and the channel distribution information's imperfection (e.g., bounded moment or support errors). The core objective is to develop a tractable robust optimization methodology that jointly immunizes communication system designs—such as beamforming or resource allocation—against this composite uncertainty. This provides a holistic guarantee of performance and reliability under a broader, more realistic class of channel imperfections than either approach addresses in isolation.

ideas.title: Asymptotically Optimal Transceiver Design for a Generalized Class of Utility Functions in Massive MIMO
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the fundamental limits and optimal transceiver structures for massive MIMO systems under a generalized class of utility functions and performance metrics. By leveraging random matrix theory and asymptotic analysis as the number of antennas grows large, the work aims to prove that certain precoding/detection schemes converge to optimality for a wide set of metrics, not just a single one. This would provide a unified design framework that is both analytically tractable and practically relevant for next-generation networks with heterogeneous service requirements. The core novelty lies in characterizing the 'asymptotic universality' of transceiver designs across diverse performance objectives.

ideas.description: This research addresses the critical flaw of static CSI feedback designs by developing a meta-learning framework for adaptive feedback strategy selection. The system continuously monitors channel non-stationarities and network state, using this data to rapidly adapt the compression, quantization, and reporting mechanisms. This ensures feedback efficiency and downlink performance are robust to changes in user mobility, scattering environment, and hardware conditions. The outcome is a self-optimizing CSI feedback loop that maintains high spectral efficiency without manual reconfiguration.

ideas.title: Robust Adaptive Control via Stochastic Dual Optimization with Performance Guarantees
ideas.description: This research develops a control-theoretic framework for minimizing power under dynamic performance targets in uncertain computing systems. It formulates the problem as a stochastic dual optimization with chance constraints, ensuring performance targets are met with a specified probability despite model inaccuracies and noise. A model predictive controller solves this problem online, using real-time telemetry to adaptively estimate the stochastic power-performance frontier and adjust control knobs (e.g., voltage, frequency, core allocation). The result is a system that provably delivers robust, near-minimal power consumption for time-varying workloads on real hardware with inherent variability.

ideas.title: Employ Percolation Theory from Statistical Physics to Model Secure Connectivity in Large-Scale D2D Networks
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from percolation theory in statistical physics to analyze secrecy in large-scale D2D networks. The analogous problem is understanding the phase transition for global connectivity in a random medium, solved by finding the percolation threshold using models like continuum percolation on Poisson point processes. This solution is adapted by defining a 'secure link' in the D2D network based on a secrecy outage probability constraint. The research will use stochastic geometry to model the spatial distributions of legitimate devices and eavesdroppers, then apply percolation-theoretic concepts to derive the critical conditions (e.g., minimum device density or transmit power) under which a giant component of securely connected devices emerges, characterizing the fundamental limits of large-scale network secrecy.

ideas.title: A Rigorous Benchmarking Framework for Metaheuristics: Isolating Algorithmic Efficacy via Systematic Tuning and Problem-Feature Analysis
ideas.description: This research establishes a rigorous methodology for comparing metaheuristics like PSO, genetic algorithms, and simulated annealing. It moves beyond naive comparisons by first constructing a benchmark suite annotated with problem features (e.g., modality, basin linearity) and then using automated algorithm configuration tools to find robust, high-performance settings for each solver under a fixed computational budget. The core analysis employs data profiles to measure the probability of each tuned algorithm solving a problem to a target accuracy within a given function evaluation count, enabling statistically sound performance comparisons. The final outcome is a nuanced understanding of which algorithm families excel on problems with specific structural characteristics, providing actionable guidance for practitioners.

ideas.title: Applying Thermodynamic Entropy Ordering via Majorization to Matrix Optimization
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from statistical mechanics and thermodynamics, where majorization theory is used to formalize the concept of disorder (entropy) and state evolution. The analogous solution uses the majorization partial order on probability vectors (e.g., of energy level populations) and Schur-convex functions to model thermodynamic constraints. Adapted to the research context, we treat the vector of eigenvalues (or singular values) of a matrix as the fundamental object for ordering. By applying majorization theory to these spectral vectors, we can define a generalized matrix ordering that extends beyond simple Loewner ordering. This spectral majorization order can then be integrated into the monotonic optimization framework: constraints can be expressed as sets of matrices whose eigenvalue vectors majorize or are majorized by a given vector, and objective functions can be designed to be Schur-convex or Schur-concave in the eigenvalues. This enables the handling of a broader class of matrix optimization problems, particularly in fields like signal processing, quantum information (pre-2026), and covariance estimation, where spectral properties are central.

ideas.title: Continuous Power-Rate Adaptation via Gradient-Based Feedback, Inspired by Continuous Control Theory
ideas.description: This idea challenges the discrete power-rate assumption by drawing an analogy from continuous control theory. In control systems, moving from discrete to continuous actuator states is solved via gradient-based feedback laws for smooth, optimal adjustment. Adapted to wireless communications, the research proposes a system where the receiver computes the gradient of a continuous network utility function with respect to transmit power and spectral efficiency. This gradient information is quantized and fed back, allowing the transmitter to perform continuous, real-time adaptations of its power and coding rate, effectively tracking the continuous capacity boundary rather than hopping between a finite set of discrete operating points. This approach promises higher efficiency and stability in time-varying channels.

ideas.title: Adapting Crystallographic Symmetry Principles for Interference Alignment
ideas.description: This idea applies concepts from crystallography and materials science to interference alignment in multi-user networks. In crystallography, complex periodic atomic structures are efficiently described using group theory and a minimal set of symmetry operations (like rotations and translations) that generate the entire crystal from a unit cell. Analogously, we can treat the desired interference pattern at each receiver as a structured 'lattice' in the signal space. The adapted solution is to design precoders not just to achieve diagonal structures, but to function as symmetry operators that systematically generate these optimal interference structures across all users. This transforms the precoder design problem into finding the minimal set of linear transformations (precoders) that, through their combined 'symmetry' actions, align interference onto the intended subspaces, potentially simplifying design and improving performance in complex network topologies.

ideas.title: Adapting Robust Statistical Mechanics for Non-Gaussian Interference in MIMO Systems
ideas.description: This idea applies analogical reasoning from statistical physics to the original challenge of matrix-monotonic optimization under non-Gaussian noise. In statistical physics, complex systems with non-Gaussian disorder (e.g., spin glasses with heavy-tailed couplings) are analyzed using robust statistical mechanics frameworks that move beyond Gaussian approximations, employing tools like stable distributions and replica methods. The analogous solution is adapted to wireless communications by developing a new matrix-monotonic optimization theory for MIMO systems where interference is modeled by non-Gaussian statistical models (e.g., alpha-stable distributions). The adapted research involves deriving novel matrix inequalities, capacity bounds, and optimal resource allocation strategies (like generalized water-filling) that are robust to and specifically designed for these non-Gaussian statistical characteristics of noise and interference.
