ideas.description: This research investigates physical layer security in wireless networks where legitimate users are mobile, and a passive eavesdropper possesses perfect Channel State Information (CSI) for all links, including those to the mobile users. The core challenge is to characterize the worst-case secrecy performance over time, as user mobility induces predictable or stochastic variations in channel states that the eavesdropper can perfectly track. The synergy lies in developing resource allocation and beamforming strategies that are robust not only to a static worst-case eavesdropper but to its dynamically optimal interception strategy across the users' trajectories, fundamentally shifting security design from a reactive to a predictive, trajectory-aware framework.

ideas.title: Robust Continuous Power-Rate Adaptation with Imperfect Channel State Information
ideas.description: This research develops a continuous power and rate adaptation system that is robust to practical channel state information imperfections. The framework incorporates a predictive model of channel evolution to mitigate feedback delay and uses optimized differential quantization for efficient finite-rate feedback. A key innovation is a safety-aware adaptation policy that constrains continuous adjustments to ensure the system's throughput or outage probability never underperforms a conservative discrete adaptation baseline, guaranteeing robustness while pursuing the gains of continuous adaptation.

ideas.title: Finite-to-Asymptotic Transition: Robust Optimal Structures for Practical Massive MIMO Scaling
ideas.description: This research investigates the design of optimal signal processing and system structures for massive MIMO that are robust across the entire practical scaling path from tens to hundreds of antennas. Instead of purely asymptotic analysis, it develops a finite-dimensional scaling law framework that explicitly accounts for key non-idealities: spatial channel correlation, hardware impairments, and limited training resources. The goal is to derive structures (e.g., for precoding, channel estimation, and resource allocation) that provide provable performance guarantees at every finite system size and converge gracefully to known asymptotic limits, ensuring practical utility for real-world deployments.

ideas.title: Robust Interference Alignment via Uncertainty-Aware Precoder Design
ideas.description: This research develops a robust interference alignment framework for multi-user MIMO networks that is resilient to imperfect channel state information. The core idea is to design precoders and receive filters that optimize a performance metric, such as worst-case sum rate or mean squared error, over a bounded set of possible channel realizations defined by CSI error statistics. The approach leverages convex optimization and chance-constrained programming to find diagonal or structured precoding solutions that guarantee alignment quality despite practical estimation and feedback limitations. This results in a system design that maintains significant interference reduction and multiplexing gains under realistic, non-ideal operating conditions.

ideas.title: Adaptive Full-Duplex Jamming with Bio-Inspired Feedback Control for Secrecy Rate Maximization
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from biomedical engineering—specifically, the adaptive self-interference cancellation techniques used in closed-loop deep brain stimulation implants—to the problem of full-duplex jamming for physical layer security in D2D networks. In neural implants, the system must record faint neural signals while simultaneously delivering powerful electrical pulses, requiring sophisticated real-time filtering to isolate the desired signal from the self-generated interference. Analogously, a D2D transmitter using full-duplex to jam an eavesdropper must transmit its information signal while generating a jamming signal, creating severe self-interference. The adapted idea proposes implementing a bio-inspired, adaptive feedback control loop at the D2D transmitter. This system would continuously estimate the eavesdropper's channel and use an adaptive algorithm to shape the jamming signal's characteristics (e.g., power, frequency, phase) in real-time. The goal is to maximize the jamming effect on the eavesdropper while using predictive SIC to minimize the degradation of its own reception, thereby dynamically optimizing the secrecy capacity of the D2D link.

ideas.title: A Unified Statistical Manifold Framework for Imperfect CSI
ideas.description: This research proposes to develop a generalized imperfect CSI model by drawing an analogy from statistical thermodynamics. In that field, diverse non-equilibrium states are unified under a single statistical manifold framework, where specific conditions correspond to submanifolds defined by constraints. The adapted solution is to construct a statistical manifold where each point represents a probability distribution of the channel estimate. The three specific imperfect CSI cases (e.g., delayed, quantized, noisy) are modeled as distinct submanifolds within this space, characterized by their specific operational constraints (like feedback delay or codebook size). A unifying principle, such as minimizing the divergence from perfect CSI or maximizing an information-theoretic utility, generates the generalized model, from which the three specific cases can be systematically derived as projections or restrictions onto their respective constraint submanifolds.

ideas.title: Secrecy Capacity under Coalitional Game Theory and Adversarial Network Formation
ideas.description: This research seed expands the analysis of secrecy performance with multiple eavesdroppers by drawing an analogy from ecology and evolutionary game theory. In ecological networks, the stability of systems like plant-pollinator webs is studied using coalitional game theory to model how species form cooperative alliances, affecting overall network resilience. Adapting this solution, we propose to model eavesdroppers as strategic players who can form dynamic coalitions to pool intercepted data, rather than being simply independent or fully collaborative. The adapted idea is to apply a coalitional game-theoretic framework to analyze how the formation, stability, and dissolution of such adversarial coalitions redefine the secrecy capacity region and system design requirements, providing a more nuanced model of multi-adversary threats.

ideas.title: Game-Theoretic Defense Against Strategic Malicious D2D Users with Adaptive Trust Management
ideas.description: This research investigates D2D network security under the threat of rational, strategic internal adversaries who can dynamically alter their behavior to evade static detection systems. It develops a dynamic Bayesian game model where each D2D pair's trustworthiness is a hidden state, continuously estimated via observable communication actions and physical layer features. The system co-designs adaptive resource allocation, cryptographic primitives, and incentive mechanisms to establish a provably secure equilibrium, ensuring that even sophisticated malicious users find compliance more beneficial than attack. Performance is evaluated in terms of resilience against collusion, learning-based mimicry attacks, and the overall impact on network efficiency and legitimate user secrecy rates.

ideas.title: Adapting Evolutionary Game Theory from Ecology for Stable Secrecy-Rate Allocations
ideas.description: This idea expands the original game-theoretic model for competitive secrecy-rate allocation by drawing an analogy from Theoretical Ecology. The analogous problem is understanding how competing behavioral strategies (like aggression vs. retreat) stabilize in animal populations, solved using Evolutionary Game Theory (EGT) and the concept of Evolutionary Stable Strategies (ESS). We adapt this by modeling D2D and cellular users as a population of agents who stochastically adopt transmission strategies (e.g., power levels, resource block choices) based on the observed secrecy-rate payoffs of others. Their strategy distribution evolves over time through imitation or replication dynamics. The adapted research idea uses this framework to analyze the convergence and stability of secrecy-rate maximization outcomes, aiming to design local interaction rules and payoff structures that guide the entire network towards a desirable ESS, providing a robust, decentralized solution for sustainable physical-layer security in heterogeneous networks.

ideas.title: Optimal Matrix Structures via Spectral Decomposition Generalization
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the common diagonalizability assumption for matrix variables in optimization and control. Drawing an analogy from quantum chemistry's solution for strongly correlated electron systems—where non-diagonalizable effective Hamiltonians are addressed via multi-configurational complete active space (CAS) methods—we propose a novel framework. The adapted idea investigates constructing optimal matrix variables by identifying a critical invariant subspace where non-diagonalizability is necessary and performance-critical. Within this 'active' subspace, the matrix structure would be optimized as a combination of Jordan blocks or other non-diagonal canonical forms, while the remainder of the matrix adopts a simpler, diagonalizable structure. This approach generalizes spectral decomposition, providing a principled method to relax the diagonalizability assumption while maintaining analytical and computational tractability for problems in systems theory, machine learning, and semidefinite programming.

ideas.title: Security-Aware Transceiver Design for D2D Networks with Low-Resolution Hardware and Internal Adversaries
ideas.description: This research investigates the unique security vulnerabilities that arise in Device-to-Device (D2D) networks when low-resolution digital-to-analog and analog-to-digital converters (DACs/ADCs) are employed for cost and power efficiency. It specifically models scenarios where some D2D pairs are untrusted internal eavesdroppers or active jammers, whose malicious behavior is compounded and obscured by the inherent non-linear quantization distortion of the low-resolution hardware. The core synergy is the development of a unified analytical framework and signal processing strategies that jointly mitigate hardware impairments and enhance physical-layer security, designing precoding, power allocation, and artificial noise schemes that account for how quantization noise alters the interference landscape and can be leveraged by adversaries. This leads to robust transceiver designs that secure communications against internal threats even under severe hardware constraints, a problem not addressed by studying security or hardware impairments in isolation.

ideas.title: Adapting Byzantine Fault Tolerance to Secure D2D Networks Against Malicious Internal Actors
ideas.description: This research investigates system security in D2D networks where some pairs are untrusted or act as internal eavesdroppers. Drawing an analogy from Byzantine Fault Tolerance in distributed computing, which solves the problem of maintaining consensus with arbitrarily faulty nodes, we adapt its core principles. The adapted idea is to design a D2D communication protocol that treats D2D pairs as nodes in a Byzantine-resilient network. By employing techniques like multi-path relay selection with redundancy, threshold cryptography for key distribution, and verification mechanisms requiring consensus among a quorum of helper nodes, the system can isolate and mitigate the impact of malicious internal D2D users, ensuring secure data transmission even in the presence of a bounded number of adversaries.

ideas.title: Asymptotic Optimal Structures in Massive MIMO via Statistical Mechanics and Mean-Field Theory
ideas.description: The original idea investigates the asymptotic optimal structures in massive MIMO as the number of antennas grows very large. Drawing an analogy from statistical mechanics, where the behavior of systems with a vast number of interacting particles (the analogous problem) is solved using mean-field theory and analysis of the thermodynamic limit. This analogous solution is adapted by applying a mean-field theoretical framework to the massive MIMO channel and signal processing problem. The research will model the antenna array in the limit N → ∞, transforming stochastic channel matrices and interference effects into deterministic equivalent operators or fields. The goal is to derive the fundamental, simplified structures for optimal beamforming and detection that emerge in this asymptotic regime, characterizing the scaling laws and identifying any sharp transitions in optimal strategy, much like phase transitions in physical systems.

ideas.title: Optimal Control Design with Guaranteed Performance for Dynamical Systems with Non-Diagonalizable Linearizations
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for optimal control and robust filter design in systems where the state matrix is not diagonalizable, a common scenario in systems with repeated poles or high-dimensional symmetry that leads to defective or nearly defective Jacobians. The core approach is to derive convex reformulations of Lyapunov inequalities and linear matrix inequalities (LMIs) that explicitly parameterize solutions using the Jordan form, moving beyond mere existence proofs to computable conditions. This enables the synthesis of controllers with rigorous stability and performance guarantees (e.g., H-infinity norms) for these challenging system classes, directly applicable to mechanical networks, quantum control, and certain large-scale interconnected systems. The outcome is a suite of numerical algorithms and a theoretical characterization of how non-normality and defectiveness fundamentally limit and shape achievable control performance.

ideas.title: Compressive Sensing for Low-Resolution Transceiver Design
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogy from medical imaging and signal acquisition—specifically compressed sensing—to the problem of transceiver design with hardware impairments from low-resolution data converters. In compressed sensing, signals are recovered from sub-Nyquist, often non-linear measurements by exploiting sparsity, enabling simpler hardware. Adapting this solution, we propose to treat a communication transceiver with severely low-resolution (1-3 bit) DACs and ADCs not as a conventional system with additive quantization noise, but as a compressive measurement system. The research would develop a new framework for modulation, coding, and non-linear signal reconstruction that explicitly leverages the inherent sparsity or structure of communication signals (e.g., in multi-carrier systems or with intermittent traffic) to maintain reliable communication despite the extreme information loss from coarse quantization, thereby enabling ultra-low-power and high-speed transceiver designs previously considered infeasible.

ideas.title: Robust Detection with Imperfect CSI via Control-Theoretic Observer Design
ideas.description: This idea challenges the perfect receiver CSI assumption by drawing an analogy from control systems engineering. In that field, the problem of controlling a system with imperfect state measurements is solved using observer or state estimator design (e.g., Kalman filters). The analogous solution is adapted to wireless communications by treating the communication channel as a dynamic system with uncertainty. The proposed research would develop receiver algorithms that function as joint channel-and-data observers. These algorithms would use a dynamic channel model and treat the available imperfect (quantized, noisy, outdated) CSI as a partial, noisy measurement. The observer would then produce refined, real-time estimates of both the transmitted symbols and the channel state, leading to more robust detection performance under realistic, imperfect CSI conditions.

ideas.title: Signal-Aware Dynamic Linearization for Low-Resolution Transceivers
ideas.description: This research designs a transceiver system that dynamically compensates for the signal-dependent non-linearities of low-resolution data converters. Instead of a static impairment model, it employs a feedback loop where the receiver characterizes the current quantization distortion using pilots and updates a digital pre-distorter at the transmitter and a corresponding non-linear equalizer at the receiver. The approach co-designs modulation, predistortion, and estimation to maintain reliable communication despite aggressive bit-depth reduction, making low-resolution hardware viable for wideband, adaptive systems like massive MIMO and full-duplex radios.

ideas.title: Adaptive Wavefront Shaping for RIS via Spatial Light Modulator Optimization
ideas.description: This idea scales the framework to optimize the phase-shift matrix of a reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) by drawing an analogy from adaptive optics in astronomy. In that field, wavefront distortion from atmospheric turbulence is corrected in real-time using a deformable mirror or spatial light modulator, which applies a conjugate phase pattern based on sensor feedback. We adapt this solution by treating the RIS as a programmable electromagnetic phase modulator. A feedback loop using channel state information from receivers will drive an optimization algorithm to iteratively adjust the RIS phase-shift matrix. The goal is to apply the conjugate of the channel's phase distortion, thereby 'focusing' or steering the reflected signal to maximize performance metrics like SNR or spectral efficiency, directly parallel to how adaptive optics systems restore a clear astronomical image.
